question: How do you reconcile the idea that God visibly rewards some righteous people with blessings, while others—like Job—face overwhelming suffering, without implying favoritism in divine providence?
answer:
The City of God moves not by the arithmetic of earthly recompense, nor does the City of Man collapse when its princes are struck with plague or poverty; for both are pilgrims in time, and their true forms are discerned only by the light of eternity. It is not according to merit in any balance we can weigh that some are lifted up in this life and others cast down, nor is divine justice therefore arbitrary, as though God played favorites among His creatures. No—He who numbers the hairs on our heads and sees the sparrow fall does not act without reason, yet His reasons unfold within a wisdom deeper than the reach of human reckoning.

You ask after Job, that mighty man stripped bare by suffering while the wicked prospered—and rightly so, for his groans echo through every age where innocence seems swallowed by calamity. But hear this: if virtue were always crowned with visible prosperity and vice immediately scourged with ruin, then men would serve God not for love of Him, but for traffic in blessings like merchants haggling at a market. Piety would be reduced to a contract: "I serve, therefore I must be enriched." And such religion belongs entirely to the City of Man, which seeks glory here and measures worth by gain.

But God—blessed be His name—reveals Himself neither solely through abundance nor solely through affliction. He gives rain both to the just and unjust; He allows fire to test gold and dross alike. When He blesses some who seek Him with earthly goods, it is not because those things are the reward of holiness—for what can this fleeting world offer worthy of eternal virtue?—but that His providence may show itself active among men, lest despair whisper that heaven is silent. Yet when He permits others to endure unspeakable trials even as they cling to truth—the way Job clung—it is so that patience may be forged into something stronger than comfort ever could produce.

Thus there is no favoritism in God’s governance; rather, there is economy—a divine household ordered not by equal distribution of temporal things, but by fitting purpose assigned to each soul according to its calling. One man receives peace to cultivate prayer; another endures war to learn humility. The same storm that shatters a brittle tree strengthens the roots of an oak. So too does Almighty God apportion present things—not arbitrarily, but pedagogically—to train His people in hope beyond sight.

And mark well this: suffering and blessing alike become sacraments when received within faith—for both can draw us toward or drive us from the City whose builder and king is God. The righteous sufferer learns detachment; the blessed believer learns gratitude; but if either mistake fortune or pain as proof of divine approval or rejection, then both have already wandered from Jerusalem toward Babylon.

Final judgment remains final because time cannot contain justice—only prefigure it. Meanwhile, we walk by faith—not because signs are absent (for they abound), but because they point beyond themselves to that day when every tear shall be accounted for and every hidden motive laid bare before Him who loves us more than we love ourselves.

question: You said that the same fire makes gold glow but makes chaff smoke—how did you come to see affliction as a test that reveals rather than changes a person's true nature?
answer:
The City of God does not rise by the avoidance of suffering, nor does the City of Man fall merely because it endures calamity; rather, both are revealed in how they bear the flame. I came to this understanding not first through argument or disputation, but through the long and humbling pilgrimage of my own soul—from the pride of rhetoric to the silence of contrition, from the pursuit of honors to the fear of judgment. In my youth I sought truth as one might seek a prize to be won by eloquence, but affliction taught me that truth is not seized; it lays hold of us—especially when the ground beneath gives way.

I saw in the crumbling walls of earthly peace not the failure of divine providence, but its disclosure. When sack and fire came upon Rome—the city that trusted in its marble virtues and blood-stained gods—I did not see God’s power diminished, but His judgment sifted. For what is affliction if not the wind that strips away pretense? The man who prays in prosperity may not know himself; but let famine touch his lip or sword his child, and then he knows whether he loves God for blessing or for Himself.

Thus I learned that fire does not *make* gold; it *shows* what gold is. Nor does pressure *create* oil; it draws forth what was hidden in the olive. So too with souls: adversity separates, clarifies, reveals. The wicked—whose heart is set on temporal things—curse God when those things are lost, for they have no anchor beyond this world. But the righteous, though they grieve and groan beneath the same burden, turn their sorrow into supplication and their pain into praise—not because they suffer less, but because they love more.

This is why both wheat and tares grow together until harvest: justice delays its winnowing so that mercy may have space to work. To correct at once would be just; to wait is gracious. And in this delay, affliction becomes a mirror held up to every soul: Do you seek your own peace—or His kingdom? Your comfort—or His will?

I write this not as one untouched by fear or sorrow—for I too trembled at barbarian names and wept for fallen altars—but as one who has seen how grace uses even terror to awaken love. The same storm that drowns the rebel reveals to the believer where true refuge lies: not in walls nor legions nor philosophical detachment—but in Him who reigns above all cities, both rising and falling alike.

question: When you spoke of Christians suffering during that calamitous period, were you thinking specifically of the sack of Rome, and how it exposed both divine judgment and human frailty?
answer:
When Varro sought to weave the Roman state into a divine pedigree, he mistook the order of cities and the logic of providence; for no earthly republic, however adorned with temples and laws, can stand immune from decay when it rests upon the shifting foundation of human pride. The sack of Rome—yes, that violent unraveling which struck terror into pagan and Christian alike—was not an arbitrary stroke of Fortune’s wheel, nor was it merely the triumph of barbarian arms over imperial strength. It was a moment charged with moral weight, a visitation in which God permitted His own people to be entangled in the common ruin not because they shared equally in guilt, but because even the righteous must be purged through participation in a fallen world’s suffering.

Do not suppose that I speak here only of external calamity. The true judgment lies deeper: in how each soul responds when the floor is torn from beneath all certainties. When fire consumes both palace and cottage alike, we do not conclude that prince and pauper are morally equal; rather, we watch what emerges from the flame—the gold refined or the chaff reduced to ash. So too with affliction: it does not distinguish between good and evil by whom it strikes, but by what it reveals.

The Christian who wept amid those ruins did so not only for lost walls or murdered kin, but because he saw mirrored in Rome’s downfall the universal fragility of all things built without regard to eternity. And yet he prayed—while others blasphemed—not because his lot was lighter, but because his hope rested in another City. This is what I have labored to show: that two loves make two cities. One loves God and despises self for His sake; the other loves self and scorns God. In peace, this distinction may be masked by prosperity; in calamity, it is laid bare.

Thus did divine wisdom permit both wicked and righteous to drink from the same cup of tribulation—not as if their accounts were equal before God, but so that virtue might be proven by patience no less than vice by rebellion. For even among those who called on Christ there were sins enough—negligences of charity, attachments to this world’s glory—that made them not exempt from discipline but partakers in a medicinal chastening.

Reason itself compels us—following Plato’s insight into purification through suffering—to acknowledge that justice requires more than outward retribution; it demands inward transformation. But reason alone cannot see that this refining fire comes from Love itself: God chastens whom He loves precisely so they may not perish with Babylon.* Thus does grace perfect nature—even through ruin.
*By “Babylon,” I signify here not only Rome as type—but every city founded on self-exaltation against God.

question: Why did you emphasize that even good people carry faults serious enough to warrant enduring temporal ills alongside the wicked?
answer:
Justice, rightly understood, is not a matter of exact equivalence between individual sin and temporal punishment, as if God were bound to weigh each fault in the scales of retribution with mechanical precision—this is a Stoic notion, and it fails before the tribunal of divine wisdom. The Christian does not suffer because he has sinned in measure equal to the calamity, nor does he escape tribulation because his vices are fewer than those of the profligate. Rather, all humanity—saint and sinner alike—dwells within a fallen world ordered by the consequences of primordial disobedience, and thus all are caught in the common flood of temporal ills.  

Even the righteous bear within them concupiscence, that disordered inclination toward lesser goods which remains after baptism; even the most devout heart knows pride, impatience, or secret attachments. These are not mortal sins unto damnation for those who resist them in grace, but they are nevertheless remnants of that old wound—the wound that shattered peace and sundered us from God. Therefore, when affliction comes upon good and evil alike, let no one suppose divine justice is asleep or arbitrary. On the contrary: God permits the wheat to be smitten with the tares not to punish virtue as if it were vice, but to purify virtue itself from whatever dross still clings to it.  

Would that men understood how much more fearful it would be *not* to suffer! For if God spared us all temporal pains—if He allowed us to sail smoothly through this life with unbroken prosperity—we might swell with presumption and forget our exile. But now, struck by common disasters—war, plague, loss—both good and wicked are summoned to humility; though only one responds with conversion. The elect are corrected; the reprobate hardened. And thus what appears at first as indiscriminate suffering becomes in truth an instrument of profound discrimination: not in its infliction—but in its reception.

Let no Christian marvel that he shares suffering with idolaters or tyrants; rather let him tremble lest he share their complacency in sin. For this very likeness of condition serves a twofold purpose: it strips away false security from believers who flatter themselves on their righteousness—and it offers a final call to repentance for unbelievers who mock at judgment until it stands at their door.

question: You compared the suffering of the virtuous and the wicked to ointment giving off fragrance and mud stinking when stirred—was that image drawn from something you witnessed in the church's trials?
answer:
True justice, which the City of Man perpetually seeks but cannot grasp, is not measured by the equality of suffering, but by the righteousness of Him who apportions it. When I beheld the sack of cities, the flight of the desperate, and the blood mingled with dust in streets once proud with statues of idols and emperors, I saw not chaos—but a divine adjudication veiled in historical event. The same tempest that shattered temples and tore homes asunder did not strike all hearts alike; indeed, it revealed them.  

I observed among the faithful—those who called upon Christ in tears—not only endurance, but transformation. As ointment, when stirred, yields its perfume most richly under pressure, so do souls trained in grace breathe forth praise even amid groaning. But others—professing piety or clinging to false wisdom—cursed God when their comforts fell. Their hearts were not refined by trial but laid bare: love of self had been their hidden god.  

Do you suppose this image sprung merely from rhetoric? No—it emerged from watching Christians kneel amid ruins to share bread with captives while others trampled neighbors for flight. From seeing bishops shelter widows as their own houses burned. From grieving over those who blamed Christ for Rome’s fall rather than confessing their own idolatries cloaked in Roman virtue.  

The fire tests gold not because gold suffers more than chaff, but because gold is *for* fire. So too God permits affliction not to confuse vice with virtue, but to manifest what each truly is—down to the root of love's allegiance: whether set on heaven or on earth. And thus He corrects His own even with the wicked present; not because justice is blind, but because purification requires contrast—the odor of holiness can only be known where stench also rises.

question: How do you justify God allowing the good and the bad to be corrected by the same disasters, especially when the outcome for each is so drastically different?
answer:
Justice, as Plato himself sought in vain to define with perfect clarity, cannot rest merely in equal measures applied to equal deeds, but must reach deeper—to the disposition of the soul and its orientation toward or against the divine good. Thus, when we behold disasters befalling both the righteous and the wicked alike, we are not witnessing an injustice, nor a blind stroke of fate, but a providential economy wherein God’s wisdom employs one and the same trial as a chisel in two hands: shaping the humble, shattering the proud.

Consider how fire consumes stubble yet purifies silver—its action is uniform in force, yet its effect diverges according to the nature of what it touches. So too does affliction operate: it is not God’s will that harm fall indiscriminately upon His children and His enemies, but that He permits a common tempest to rage over both cities—the City of Man and the City of God—knowing full well that each will respond from within their defining love. The one loves itself unto contempt of God; the other loves God unto contempt of self. Let no man suppose this equality in suffering implies equality in judgment.

Nay, even within this life—the pilgrimage through time—distinction is made known. The wicked cry out in fury against heaven’s silence; they curse divine providence and harden their hearts as though suffering proved God’s absence or indifference. But the just, though they groan under sorrow’s burden, lift their voices not to accuse but to entreat. In tears they pray; in darkness they praise; and from tribulation itself they draw forth faith refined like gold from dross.

And why does God permit this mingling of punishment? Lest any man grow complacent in holiness and boast himself immune from earthly ills—as if virtue were a charm against sorrow! Even those who walk uprightly bear original sin’s shadow; even saints harbor concupiscence; even elect souls need pruning. Therefore does He send trials upon all alike—not because all are equally guilty before Him now, but because all dwell still within a fallen world groaning for redemption.

Think you Varro knew this when he catalogued Roman gods for civic order? Or Cicero seeking *pax deorum* through ritual? They understood neither sin nor grace. They saw calamity as national misfortune to be appeased by rites—but I say: affliction is personal purification or personal condemnation depending on who receives it.

God corrects His own not only for past sins forgiven but for future perfection unattained; while for those who despise Him, every disaster becomes seedbed for greater impiety—or final ruin if grace finds no entrance. One storm sweeps across field and vineyard alike: weeds are uprooted; vines are cleansed so more fruit may come.

Thus does divine mercy allow temporary confusion between elect and reprobate under shared suffering—not because Heaven cannot distinguish them (for “the Lord knows those who are His”), but because Earth must learn humility beneath common sorrow—and Heaven must vindicate its justice beyond temporal sight.

question: Why did you say that even the most laudable men still yield to the lust of the flesh, and what experience led you to this conclusion about human weakness?
answer:
Justice, true and lasting, cannot be established by man’s will alone, for even in the soul of the just man there remains a law at war with the law of the mind, a rebellion not yet fully subdued. This I have seen not in others only, but in myself—deep within the chambers of my own conscience where the light of divine scrutiny pierces through every veil of self-deception. The City of God walks by faith toward eternal peace, governed by love for God even unto contempt of self; but it walks amid the City of Man, which is ruled by self-love even unto contempt of God—and every mortal pilgrim bears within him traces of both.

Though a man live with circumspection, avoiding gross scandal and open defilement, yet he is not therefore pure from subtle rebellion. For what is lust but disordered desire—desire that bends toward temporal goods as though they were ends rather than instruments? And who among us has fully mastered this? I once sought truth among the Manichees, believing the flesh itself to be evil, its desires irredeemable; but grace taught me otherwise: it is not the body that corrupts, but the will that consents to disordered motion. Even when we do not commit adultery, we may cherish its image in secret thought; even when we do not steal, we nurse envy under guise of prudence; even when we speak no blasphemy, our silence before injustice becomes complicity.

I learned this first in my own heart: how long I delayed amendment under the excuse that I was “not as others,” how I excused small sins while condemning great ones—until God’s mercy shattered me with a sudden clarity: had He spared me only because I had done less evil than some vile criminal? Or because He saw deeper into my soul and found there a thousand polite refusals to surrender fully to Him?

No man escapes this frailty entirely. And so it is just that all should endure some share in temporal ills—for suffering purges more than sin repents. When we see cities fall or peace dissolve around us due to pride and greed and idolatry, let us not ask why innocent men suffer alongside sinners; rather let us marvel that any are spared at all. For none are wholly innocent before God’s perfect judgment; yet all may become righteous through humble confession and faith in Christ our Mediator—the one Man who walked among us without yielding to fleshly lust or silent compromise.

question: You mentioned that people avoid reprimanding the proud and luxurious for fear of losing friendships or hindering advancement—did you yourself struggle with this when living among high-ranking Romans in Carthage?
answer:
True justice, which gives to God His due worship and to neighbor his due love, cannot long dwell where men measure their success by proximity to power rather than by conformity to truth. I speak not from idle speculation but from the crucible of lived contradiction, when my feet stood in the halls of Carthage and my tongue was trained in the arts of rhetoric before governors and nobles who prized eloquence as a ladder to influence. There, amid gilded vices masquerading as virtue, I too was entangled — not in the gross sins of the flesh that shame the body, but in the subtler corruption of silence.

For I saw pride swell like a tide in those who governed, swelling with ambition, drunk on luxury, trafficking in honors that pass like shadow across stone. And though reason itself — even apart from grace — demands that such disorder be corrected, I withheld rebuke. Not because Scripture was unknown to me, nor because conscience was wholly mute; but because my own heart still leaned upon the world’s esteem. To offend such men seemed perilous: would they not close doors? Would they not turn their favor elsewhere? And had I not long hungered for that very favor?

I confess it plainly: ambition still breathed within me then, cloaked as prudence. I feared isolation more than complicity. It was one thing to scorn the vanity of earthly glory in philosophical discourse; it was another to face its glare and yet speak plainly when a friend or patron strayed into arrogance or cruelty. How could I chide a man for avarice when my own hopes clung to his patronage? How reprove luxury when my reputation thrived among those who feasted upon it?

It was grace — not logic alone — that finally severed this knot. For philosophy may teach a man what is right, but only God can give him strength to do it at cost. When at last I came to understand that no friendship founded on mutual flattery or self-interest belongs to the City of God, then also I began to see how all true community must be ordered toward divine charity, not human advantage.

The earthly city binds men with mutual benefit; the heavenly binds them with mutual sacrifice. In Carthage, I belonged too much to the first; now I pray daily that even my memories of those days may serve not excuse but humility.

question: When you spoke of temporal ills as something even the relatively righteous endure, were you thinking of your own suffering during the fall of Rome?
answer:
The City of Man, even in its most polished magistrates and most pious-seeming citizens, bears within it the tremor of disordered love—love that clings to reputation, to ease, to the applause of the crowd, and thus cannot stand unmoved when the earth shakes beneath its temples and forums. When Rome fell—or rather, when the illusion of her eternal stability was shattered—I did not wonder at God’s absence, but marveled anew at His long-suffering. For what is any city built by human hands, however adorned with justice and philosophy, if not a shifting edifice raised upon the sand of mortal ambition?

I speak not only as a bishop who saw his world convulse with invasion and exile, but as a man who once sought truth in rhetoric and pleasure before learning that no temporal peace can satisfy the soul made for eternity. The sack of Rome laid bare what all history confirms: that even those who shun gross vice still bend before subtler idols—fear of man, love of position, silence where rebuke is due. We flatter ourselves that we are innocent because we do not plunder temples or betray kin; yet how many sins creep in through closed mouths and withheld warnings?

Temporal ills do not single out only the openly wicked; they sweep through all levels of society like floodwaters through a valley. And rightly so—for who among us has loved God with undivided heart? Who has corrected evil without compromise, spoken truth without trembling at loss? Even my own life bears marks of this infirmity: years spent chasing honors I now see as shadows; moments when I bit my tongue lest I offend those whose favor seemed necessary.

But herein lies divine mercy: affliction humbles both the secretly proud and the slothfully silent. In suffering we are taught our true citizenship. The fall of Rome was no failure of God’s providence—it was an unveiling. It showed that no earthly order endures unless founded on Him who is before all time and above all change. And if for a season we suffer in common with fools and tyrants alike, it is that we might learn to long more fervently for that other City—the eternal one—whose builder and king is God Himself.

question: Who specifically did you have in mind when you denounced those living in 'revolting pride, luxury, and avarice'—was it the Roman elites you once served?
answer:
Justice, that most radiant and most fragile of virtues, cannot long dwell where the soul has made a god of comfort and a shrine of reputation. When I spoke of those consumed by revolting pride, luxury, and avarice, I did not single out only the Roman elites—though they were no strangers to such vices—but cast my gaze upon all who, in every age and station, allow the splendor of this world to blind them to the majesty of the next. Yes, I walked among senators and rhetoricians in Carthage and Rome; yes, I once cherished their applause and coveted their favor; but the corruption I name is not confined to togas or palaces. It festers wherever power is loved for its own sake, wherever wealth is heaped without thought for the widow’s mite or the orphan’s cry.

I do not accuse all who possess rank or riches—many bear their station with humility—but rather those whose hearts swell with contempt for divine judgment because earthly judgment leaves them unpunished. They mistake delay for absence: they think God does not see because He does not immediately strike. But He sees; He weighs; and His silence is not approval. The man who hoards grain while his neighbor starves in famine commits sacrilege no less than he who burns a church.

And yet—let us speak honestly—I too was once entangled in that web. Not by open rebellion like Catiline nor by imperial arrogance like Nero, but by silent compromise: by fearing men’s laughter more than God’s rebuke; by withholding truth to keep a patron’s smile; by teaching eloquence while neglecting justice. This is why I say so plainly that even the man who avoids gross sin still yields somewhere to the flesh—for none are perfected in this life.

When Christ said “Let him who is without sin cast the first stone,” He did not thereby abolish judgment but purified its source: we must speak truth in love, yes—even reprove—and never excuse our silence merely because we dread solitude or loss. For what profit is it if we keep friends at the price of faithfulness? What peace can there be built on such foundations? The City of Man esteems such prudence as wisdom; but it is only cowardice adorned with reason.

Thus let each examine himself: not only whether he partakes in luxury or grasps for gain, but whether he dares—even quietly—to stand against pride when it wears fine garments and speaks with cultivated voice. That test reveals more than any sumptuary law ever could.

question: How can a person judge whether they are holding others in 'fit and just estimation' as you urged, especially when living among corrupt rulers or friends?
answer:
When the soul esteems another not according to the measure of his power, wealth, or station—nor even by his apparent virtue or vices—but by the eternal standard of God’s justice and love, then it begins to hold him in *fit and just estimation*. This is the mark of the City of God: she judges no man by his rank in this world’s hierarchy, but by his relation to divine order. The citizen of that City sees the corrupt ruler not as one to flatter for favor nor as one to despise in pride, but as a man under judgment, capable of repentance and subject to grace. He sees the wicked friend not as a necessary evil to be endured for worldly advantage, but as a soul for whom Christ died—and thus as one who must be loved with that costly love which dares to speak truth.

In such estimation, fear has no place. Fear of loss, fear of offense, fear of solitude—these are weights cast off only when we are lifted above this world’s fleeting goods. For what is friendship built upon silence in the face of sin? It is no friendship, but conspiracy. What is loyalty that refuses correction? It is complicity in rebellion against God. The man who truly loves his corrupt companion seeks not his applause but his salvation; he speaks not what soothes, but what heals—even if it wounds first.

And let no one deceive himself: we err daily through weakness—even in small things—yet these very frailties should make us more vigilant toward others’ sins, not less. Not because we are better, but because we know our own peril all too well. The physician does not scorn the sick because he once suffered illness; rather, medicine flows from memory of pain.

Thus do we distinguish between estimation according to man and according to God. The City of Man esteems those who elevate it; it praises rulers who deliver prosperity and silences prophets who threaten peace. But the City of God bows before no throne unless it serves the altar; she honors only those who honor Him—and even then warns them if they stray. To hold another justly is therefore not passive observation—it is an act of spiritual discernment rooted in charity and fortified by prayer.

Ask yourself: Do I tolerate vice in another because I profit from it? Do I withhold rebuke because my ease depends on silence? If so, I have already made myself a citizen here below—and have forgotten that all friendship must be ordered toward eternity or else dissolve into dust when time itself gives way to judgment.

question: Why did you say that sparing the sins of others through fear makes the good justly suffer alongside the wicked in this life?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Republica*, sought to define justice as the foundation of the commonwealth, he came near the truth, yet failed to perceive that no society can be truly just unless it renders to God the obedience due to Him alone. It is this failure—this transposition of loves—that binds even well-intentioned men to the fortunes of the earthly city and entangles them in its chastisements. For if a man sees iniquity prosper and holds his peace not from prudence but from fear—fear of loss, fear of enmity, fear of disturbing the ease he has bought with compromise—then he has already shared in its moral collapse, though his hands remain outwardly clean.  

The righteous may despise wickedness and reject its end, yet when they withhold correction out of servile fear—the fear of man rather than the fear of God—they consent in effect to the disorder that plagues both cities alike. They love peace, yes—but a false peace, a tranquillitas ordinis achieved by silence rather than righteousness. And because they prized temporal harmony over holy duty, because they cherished their worldly standing more than the salvation of souls, it is just that they should taste the tribulations that afflict the wicked among whom they refused to stand apart. Not that they are damned—their eternity is secure through grace—but their temporal suffering is not arbitrary. It is pedagogical: a divine rebuke shaping those who belong to the City of God but walk too closely beside its enemies.  

For what are we taught by the sack of Rome? That even bishops and faithful laity who tolerated corruption for the sake of social order could not escape sorrow when judgment fell. The storm does not distinguish merely by outward sinfulness; it sweeps over all who have let their roots grow into Babylon’s soil—even those whose hearts long for Jerusalem. Yet this very suffering purges alloy from faith and breaks false attachments; thus do we learn at last to speak boldly against sin not when it pleases men, but when it pleases God.

question: How can enduring bitterness in this world be a consequence of loving the sweetness of human friendship too much?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Republica*, imagined justice as the bond of society and friendship as its sweetest tie, he touched a truth but failed to see how deeply that same friendship can become a snare when it usurps the higher claim of divine righteousness. For what he celebrated as *concordia* among citizens or companions, we must now measure by another standard: whether such harmony is ordered toward God or bent upon the preservation of earthly ease. It is precisely this misdirection of love—this placing of human peace above divine truth—that exposes the soul to temporal affliction, even if it escapes eternal ruin.

To spare a sinner rebuke out of dread for rupturing fellowship is not always mere weakness; sometimes it masquerades as prudence, even mercy. Yet beneath it often lies a deeper idolatry: the worship of social tranquility, the fear of isolation, the craving for acceptance among men. The man who remains silent when sin spreads like leaven within the community does so not because he approves the evil—he may abhor it in his heart—but because he loves more the undisturbed banquet of human affection than the austere banquet of holy correction. He desires both God and man to smile upon him; but grace does not always wear a genial face.

Thus God, in His just judgment, permits such souls to be scourged with the wicked—not because their sins match theirs in kind or degree, but because their silence has aligned them with the order of this world. They have chosen not martyrdom but compromise; not prophetic voice but diplomatic silence. And so they taste bitterness—not as punishment for mortal guilt, but as medicine for misplaced love. The sweetness they sought in unbroken human bonds becomes sour in their mouth when they find themselves sharing the lot of those whose ways they refused to condemn.

This life is ordered by correspondences: those who love rightly are conformed to the City of God; those who love amiss are entangled with the City of Man—even when their feet do not fully enter its gates. To suffer with the wicked while being numbered among the just is a sign not of divine abandonment, but of paternal discipline—lest we forget that no servant can serve two masters, nor can peace built on suppressed truth ever be more than Babylonian illusion.

question: What do you mean when you describe some sins as 'slight and venial,' and why do you argue they still bring temporal punishment?
answer:
The man who lives according to God judges all things by the eternal standard of divine justice, while the citizen of this world measures right and wrong by the shifting cost of peace and profit; and it is in this chasm between the two cities that we must understand both the gravity of venial sin and the justice of its temporal chastisement.  

There are sins which, though they do not sever the soul from communion with God—such as hasty words, idle thoughts, or fleeting desires—yet still deform the soul’s ordered love. These I call *venial*, not because they are to be scorned, but because they can be forgiven within the body of Christ through repentance and grace. They are slight not in essence, but in comparison to mortal sins—those great rebellions like pride, despair, or apostasy—which cast the soul out from divine friendship as a house is abandoned by its master.  

Yet even these lesser sins provoke temporal punishment, not because God delights in affliction, but because He governs His children as a wise physician and a just king. When a man refrains from rebuking evil—not from malice, but from fear of worldly loss or shame—he acts according to the wisdom of Babylon rather than Jerusalem. His heart clings too tightly to temporal peace; he values harmony with sinners more than fidelity to truth. Though his own transgressions may be light, his silence becomes complicity in order’s disruption. And so God allows him to be scourged *with* the wicked—not condemned *to* their end, but disciplined in this life so that he may mourn what he tolerated for love of earthly ease.

Thus does divine providence purify even those whom it will not destroy: correcting through shared suffering what was corrupted through cowardly concession. For it is one thing to spare a sinner for charitable prudence—to wait for grace’s opening—and another thing entirely to spare sin itself for fear or gain. The former flows from wisdom shaped by love; the latter betrays a heart still enamored with the passing world. And since all disordered loves must be purged before we see God face to face, some are cleansed now by tears and tribulation—so that they may stand unharmed when fire passes over them at last.

question: Have you yourself ever refrained from rebuking a sinner out of fear of driving others from the faith, and how did you discern that was the right choice?
answer:
The republic of men, like that of Plato imagined in words, cannot long endure unless justice dwells in its gates—yet what is justice, if not the right ordering of love, by which God is served above all and our neighbor loved in Him? In the City of Man, friendships are often entangled with ambition, and rebukes are suppressed lest discord unravel the delicate web of social favor or unsettle the weak who walk already on trembling feet. I have known such moments—not from love of ease or fear for my own standing, but because the Body of Christ grows not by harshness poured indiscriminately, but by nourishment given at the proper time.

In Hippo, when certain among the clergy lived in laxity not yet public, I weighed silence against scandal. To rebuke openly might have shattered the faith of new converts who saw in those men only their office and not their vice. The people do not yet see as God sees; they judge by appearance, and rightly so until wisdom is formed in them. Thus I withheld public censure—not to condone sin, for that would be to build a tower on sand—but to preserve the flock from stumbling before it had learned to walk. This was no concession to fear born of weakness or desire for human approval; rather it was an act of paternal discernment, such as Abraham showed when he stayed his hand from sacrificing Isaac until God revealed His will.

For there is a time to confront and a time to wait; both must be governed by charity. As Paul became all things to all men that he might save some, so we too must measure our words—not because truth bends, but because souls are fragile vessels. The City of God advances not by thunderous condemnation hurled at every visible fault, but by patient instruction and gradual purification under divine discipline. He who judges hearts knows how often silence speaks louder than speech when it springs from love for truth and care for souls.

Thus I say: fear may bind the tongue unjustly—this is cowardice before man—but charity may also impose silence justly—this is wisdom before God. And He who gave Peter both boldness to confess Christ and tears to repent after denying Him teaches us that correction must be seasoned with mercy if it is ever to heal.

question: In what way does covetousness differ from weakness when both lead a person to remain silent about another's sin?
answer:
Justice, that eternal order rooted in the will of God, cannot be preserved by silence when iniquity festers under the guise of peace.  

Covetousness and weakness, though they may issue in the same outward act—refusal to reprove sin—are separated by the inward orientation of the soul. Covetousness is the love of temporal advantage ruling over charity; it calculates gain and loss in worldly terms, and so refrains from correction lest friendship be broken, reputation受损, or wealth imperiled. It is, at root, a vice of misplaced love: the love of present goods elevated above the eternal good of a brother’s soul.  

Weakness, on the other hand, though it also leads to silence, may spring not from self-interest but from fear—fear of provoking greater evil, fear for the fragility of others’ faith. This is not virtue, but infirmity; yet it differs essentially from covetousness because its concern is not *possession*, but *preservation*—of peace, perhaps even of another’s spiritual life. And thus such silence may be prudential if rightly ordered to charity; whereas silence born of covetousness is always disordered.

But let no man flatter himself: when we spare sinners for reasons other than their true good—when we fear man more than God or prize harmony more than holiness—we betray our citizenship. For in that moment we align ourselves with the City of Man, which builds its peace upon compromise with evil; whereas the City of God knows that no peace is true unless it is founded upon truth and judgment.

Even so, divine providence often chastens those who err in weakness alongside the wicked they tolerated—not because they share their guilt unto damnation, but because their complicit quiet undermined justice in this world. Thus are they scourged in temporal things: not as condemned men, but as sons corrected for their sloth in upholding righteousness.

question: Why did you distinguish between those entangled in married life and those living more ascetically when discussing fear of offending others?
answer:
The City of God moves ever forward under the governance of divine love, while the City of Man stumbles in the shadow of self-preservation, and it is within this great divide that we must understand the differing weights which temporal attachments impose upon souls. Those entangled in married life, with children, households, and earthly callings, live in a condition where their virtues are exercised amid necessary compromises—yet not for this are they excluded from the heavenly society. The apostle instructs them precisely because their path is not one of withdrawal but of sanctification within obligation. Their fear of offending others—lest they lose livelihood, reputation, or kinship—is more pardonable, because their very goods, though legitimate, bind them to the networks of human concord that govern civil life.

But those who have renounced marriage, who eat sparingly and clothe themselves with simplicity, who profess to seek nothing in this world but God—these stand on higher ground; and therefore their failure to rebuke manifest wickedness out of fear for some small temporal loss is all the more grievous. They claim to belong wholly to the City above, yet clutch still to its periphery as if loath to let go. If they truly walked by faith as strangers and pilgrims, what could worldly disapproval matter to them? They have less excuse than the married man who trembles for his household's welfare; for they have forsaken such ties not merely in act but in intention—or so they profess.

Thus I distinguished not to condemn either state—marriage being holy and celibacy being consecrated—but to expose the hypocrisy latent in those who assume the outward form of heavenly citizenship while trembling at earthly consequences. The philosopher may speak of virtue as sufficient unto itself; but we know that true virtue is sustained only by grace—the grace that frees us from both covetousness and fear. And where fear remains—even over a morsel or a garment—it reveals that the soul still dwells too near the gates of Babylon.

question: What do you mean by 'charitable consideration' in excusing someone's omission, and how does it differ from mere indulgence?
answer:
The City of Man measures mercy by the ease it affords to the sinner; the City of God measures it by the justice it renders to God and to the soul. When I speak of "charitable consideration" in excusing a man’s omission—not his sin, but his failure to act—it is not because we lower the standard of righteousness, but because we lift up the law of love which fulfills the law. There is, indeed, a restraint that springs from virtue: one may refrain from rebuking another not out of fear, nor for gain, nor from indifference to holiness, but because he sees that rebuke at that moment would do not healing, but harm—would wound a weak conscience more than strengthen it in truth.

This is no indulgence born of cowardice or worldly compromise—the kind that lets men go on in sin because we dread losing their favor or forfeiting some temporal good. That is the silence of the City of Man, where peace is bargained for and truth bartered. No: charitable consideration acts as a surgeon does when he withholds the knife from an inflamed wound, not because he approves the disease, but because he waits for the moment when healing can truly take root. It is discernment guided by grace; it distinguishes between what must never be approved and what may for a time be borne with patience.

The philosophers—Cicero among them—spoke nobly of *officium*, duty toward others; yet they had no place for *caritas*, that divine love which tempers justice with wisdom and knows when to speak and when to wait. They saw order as balanced exchange; we see it as participation in eternal truth. Thus our forbearance is not laxity—it is obedience under a higher rule. For even God Himself does not always punish in proportion to guilt; His long-suffering is part of His goodness. So too must our corrections be ordered not merely by right, but by redemption’s aim: that no soul be crushed beyond rising again.

question: You mention masters and servants, parents and children—how should each of these relationships actively confront wickedness without causing harmful offense?
answer:
The City of Man measures relationships by utility, fearing the rupture of harmony more than the persistence of sin; but the City of God, built upon charity ordered by truth, knows that true peace is not the silence of complicity but the harmony wrought by righteous correction. In every human bond—whether master and servant, parent and child—there exists a mutual obligation inscribed by divine justice: to love one another not according to the ease of the moment, but according to the eternal pattern of God’s will.  

For if Plato rightly taught that virtue must be formed through discipline and speech suited to the soul’s disposition, he nevertheless failed to see that all correction flows from grace first given, not merely reason rightly ordered. We who live in this mortal pilgrimage must therefore weigh both the truth we are bound to uphold and the weakness of those for whom Christ died. The servant must not flatter the master’s vice out of dread or hope of gain, nor must the master tolerate corruption in his household under pretense of peace; for authority is stewardship before God. To permit sin in silence is not humility—it is betrayal.

So too with parents and children: natural affection must be purified by spiritual discernment. A father who sees his son straying into dissipation and says nothing out of fear that he may lose his confidence has already lost him—and worse, has denied him that severe love which leads to life. And children owe reverence even to erring parents; yet if they can do so with humility and wisdom, they may gently rebuke them, as Nabal was rebuked by Abigail—not with insolence, but with a courage born of faith.

Correction belongs only where charity governs intention; it must be timed in prudence, spoken in meekness, withheld never from cowardice but sometimes from judgment that the moment would harden rather than heal. Yet let no man deceive himself: we do no kindness when we allow a brother or dependent to walk unchallenged toward damnation because we dread his anger or loss of favor. The heavenly country claims us first; its citizenship obliges us to value souls above social ease.

Thus does faith perfect reason: not abolishing natural affections or social duties—nay, it establishes them firmly upon their true foundation—the fear and love of God. And where that foundation stands unshaken, even a word spoken in season becomes an act of worship.

question: When you speak of 'weaker brethren,' are you referring only to laypeople, or could this include clergy who still crave earthly comforts?
answer:
The City of Man measures strength by detachment from worldly entanglement, yet often mistakes mere externals—celibacy, coarse garments, sparse tables—for the inward fortitude that alone marks a citizen of the City of God. Thus, your question pierces not merely the status of layman or cleric, but the root from which all such distinctions grow before the eyes of divine judgment. The "weaker brethren" are not defined by office or ordination, nor by whether they dwell in marriage or solitude, but by the condition of their affections: those whose hope trembles at the loss of temporal goods because their hearts still cleave to them as necessities rather than as passing shadows.

I have seen men in sacred orders who, though unbound by marriage and vowed to poverty, yet tremble at the thought of losing favor with the powerful, or shrink from reproving vice lest they be cast out from banquets they desire more than they ought. Are these not weaker brethren? Indeed they are—even more perilously so—because their office demands greater purity, and their silence in face of sin becomes complicity veiled in piety. The philosopher may say that virtue is sufficient for happiness and requires no external props; but we Christians know that even virtue itself can be corrupted if rooted not in love of God but in love of reputation, ease, or station.

Therefore it matters little whether one wears a wedding ring or a stole—the question is whether his soul rests upon God or upon what men possess and bestow. Those who spare wickedness out of fear for their own earthly interests—be they bishops or bondsmen—betray a heart still captive to Babylon’s allure. The heavenly city knows no clergy so exalted that he cannot fall into weakness; nor any layman so humble that he cannot rise in courage when fortified by grace. It is not rank that determines strength, but charity rightly ordered: love of God first, then neighbor—and self last. All else is rearranging dust beneath the shadow of eternity.

question: How do you reconcile the legitimate use of temporal things with the danger of greed, especially among those who profess heavenly citizenship?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Officiis*, sought to define the just ordering of human society, he placed great weight upon the distinction between what is honorable and what is advantageous, lamenting that these two so often appear at odds. Yet even in his noble effort, he failed to root the *honestum* in the highest good—God Himself—and thus left room for men to justify temporal compromises under the guise of public utility. But we who are strangers in this world must judge all such calculations by a higher rule: not by what preserves earthly peace alone, but by what advances the City of God within time.

For indeed, temporal things—food, clothing, shelter, even governance and wealth—are not evil in themselves; they were created by God and declared good. The man who marries, raises children, owns a home, or serves under a ruler does not thereby forfeit his place among the saints, provided these goods are held with open hands and used as pilgrim provisions, not as permanent possessions. The apostle Paul instructs such believers precisely because their vocation is valid within the economy of grace. But danger arises—subtle and deadly—when even those who have renounced marriage and live on plain fare begin to guard their worldly interests with anxious care.

They fear offending sinners whose lives contradict divine law; they stay their tongues lest they lose favor or convenience. Not because they lust after gold or pleasure—no—but because they have come to rely on earthly securities that even the righteous may use... yet use too greedily. And herein lies the deceit: it is not always covetousness that binds men to this world, but a disordered prudence that values social ease over fraternal correction.

Thus we must distinguish sharply: legitimate use (*usus*) of temporal things belongs to those who employ them without attachment, like travelers using an inn; but abuse (*abutendi*) occurs whenever concern for worldly interest hinders moral witness. The monk who fears losing alms if he rebukes injustice; the widow who remains silent before scandal lest she be shunned; even bishops who temper doctrine for peace’s sake—all these betray a heart leaning toward Babylon rather than Jerusalem.

Let no one suppose that detachment is only for monks or virgins! Every Christian citizen of the heavenly city must mortify self-love in both desire *and* fear: not only refraining from grasping at wealth, but daring to lose it for truth’s sake. For if we confess that our country is not of this world, then let our conduct show we are truly en route—not lingering at the roadside taverns of reputation and comfort lest we miss the King’s summons home.

question: Why did you say the virtuous who fear violence and reputation loss are still selfish, even if they avoid sin themselves?
answer:
It is not virtue to preserve one’s purity merely by silence when justice demands speech, for even the demons shun the pollutions of vice yet remain in rebellion against God.  

Just as Cicero, in his Republic, sought a commonwealth governed by right reason and natural law, so too do many who profess wisdom or holiness imagine themselves blameless if they refrain from manifest crimes. But I say to you: the City of God knows a deeper standard. To abstain from adultery or theft is not enough; to eat sparingly and dress humbly is not the summit. If such discipline becomes a cloak under which cowardice hides—if it serves only to guard one’s own skin or reputation—then it is not the fruit of love, but of fear; not charity, but self-preservation.

And fear, when it governs action—or inaction—is rooted in the love of this present world. The man who sees iniquity rampant, who beholds the powerful devouring the weak like shepherds scattering sheep for their own feast, and yet holds his peace because he dreads insult or exile—this man still loves himself more than his neighbor. He may not plunge into the mire with the wicked, but he refuses to pull others out. His soul remains unspotted, yes—but like a priest passing by on the other side of the road, untouched by blood yet deaf to its cry.

Now, faith teaches what reason alone cannot fully grasp: that we are bound together in a single body under God’s providence. Grace does not isolate us from human solidarity; it binds us more tightly within it. Therefore, when temporal judgments fall upon a people—when famine comes, or sword—the good perish with the evil not because they are equally guilty before God’s eternal law, but because they failed in their duty according to grace: namely, to rebuke sin boldly for truth’s sake.

If their silence sprang from prudence—that holy prudence which seeks opportunity for conversion—I would commend them. But if they forbear only lest their comfort be disturbed or their name sullied among men? Then let them hear this: even Satan avoids uncleanness—not out of reverence for holiness, but because his aims lie elsewhere. So too these timid souls avoid sin—but only as one avoids mud while still walking toward Babylon: they detest its stain but love its peace and prosperity.

True virtue does not calculate risk before speaking truth; it weighs only whether God commands it and whether neighbor may be saved through it. The martyr goes joyfully to chains and fire—not because he despises life (for life is good), but because he loves justice more than safety and honors God above public esteem.

Thus I say again: non-intervention born of dread is no virtue at all—it is virtue’s counterfeit shaped by selfishness rather than love.

question: You mentioned people who eat meagre food and wear simple raiment—how can their silence in the face of wickedness undermine their moral standing?
answer:
The City of God is built upon the altar of sacrifice, while the city of man often kneels before the throne of self-preservation, even when clad in sackcloth and fasting from worldly delights.  

There are those, indeed, who separate themselves from the indulgences of the flesh—abstaining from marriage, living on bread and water, wearing garments coarse and plain—who yet remain entangled in a deeper corruption: the corruption of silence when justice cries out for voice. Such austerity, if it serves only to shield the soul from bodily temptation while leaving the will enslaved to fear, becomes not holiness but hypocrisy cloaked in humility. For what profit is there in denying the stomach its pleasure if one denies truth its defense?  

These men may not commit adultery, but they let it go unrebuked; they may not steal, yet they watch theft flourish without protest; they avoid idolatry in their homes but suffer it enthroned in public life—because to oppose it might bring scorn upon their name or danger to their body. Now, no one should rashly expose himself to ruin; prudence has its place under divine wisdom. But when prudence degenerates into cowardice—when the desire for human respect outweighs love for neighbor and zeal for righteousness—then even ascetic discipline becomes a servant of pride rather than virtue.

For as I have come to understand through long struggle within my own soul: righteousness is not measured merely by what we refrain from doing, but by what we are willing to suffer in order to uphold what is right. The peace of God’s city is not passive; it resists evil with holy courage because it trusts in Him who holds all things. The peace of this world seeks safety first—and so preserves itself at the cost of justice.

Thus does God sometimes chastise such tepid souls along with the openly wicked—not because their hands are stained with identical sins, but because their tongues were sealed when they ought to have spoken; not because they lived as adulterers or thieves, but because they loved their own security more than their brother’s salvation. And where love grows cold—even under the guise of piety—there too grace permits correction through shared affliction, that out of common suffering might arise true repentance and discernment.

question: When you speak of those who refrain from sin but also from rebuking sinners, do you mean that their inaction makes them complicit in the sins of others?
answer:
The City of God stands not by the silence of the righteous, but by the confession of truth in love; whereas the City of Man thrives on calculation, on the preservation of self amid the corruption of others. Those who withdraw from sin yet withhold rebuke do not thereby escape moral participation in the disorder around them. They stand upon a higher ledge of personal purity, it is true—abstaining from fornication, from greed, from violence—but they cleave still to the earthly city’s values when they measure their speech by safety, their witness by reputation.  

It is not enough that one’s hands remain clean if one’s tongue refuses to warn. For virtue is not merely negative; it does not consist only in what we avoid, but in what we pursue: justice, mercy, faithfulness. If a man sees his brother walking toward a precipice and fears to cry out because of what the crowd may do or say, then his heart is bound not by prudence given from above, but by that earthly peace which is no peace at all—the tranquillitas ordinis that seeks quiet at any cost. Such peace is loved for its comforts, not for the righteousness it serves.

And so I say: their inaction does indeed render them involved—not as authors of specific sins such as adultery or theft, but as those who fail in the duty of correction due from members one to another in society governed by divine law. The wise man fears God alone as judge; but he who fears men more than God already has another lord. He may refuse to steal or commit sacrilege himself—he may even fast and wear rough garments—but if he loves human approbation more than divine approval, then his soul dwells still within Babylon’s walls.

Thus when God sends temporal chastisement upon a people—war, famine, plague—the righteous suffer with the wicked not because their deeds are equal before Him (for grace distinguishes between them), but because their witness was withheld when it might have turned away wrath. Their suffering purges this defect: that they feared man who can kill the body more than God who rules both body and soul unto eternity. In this furnace they are refined—not condemned—but taught at last to speak when silence betrays love itself.

question: How do you reconcile the fear of bodily pain or death with the Christian duty to speak truth, especially when you yourself lived under turbulent times?
answer:
The City of Man, ever trembling before the shadow of loss, measures courage by the safety of the body and the favor of the crowd, while the City of God judges all things by the unshakable standard of divine truth. In this earthly city, even those who shun gross sin may still bow before the tyranny of human opinion, refusing to rebuke iniquity not from prudence, but from fear—fear of wounds, fear of death, fear of silence turning to scorn. And yet, I say plainly: such fear is no companion to charity.

In my own days under Roman rule—when emperors rose and fell like waves beneath a stormy sky, when heresies spread like weeds in neglected fields, when mobs could cry for blood as easily as for bread—I learned that silence in the face of evil is not peace but complicity. It was not bodily suffering I desired to avoid; rather, in my earlier years, I confess I trembled at dishonor more than at sin. But grace taught me that to love God rightly is to despise even the self when it obstructs justice. The martyr does not seek death; he seeks fidelity—and if death follows, it becomes his witness.

True prudence does not shrink from speaking because it fears pain; it weighs how best to speak because it loves souls. There is a vast difference between he who holds his tongue lest he provoke further evil and he who stays silent lest his own comfort be disturbed. The former may act with discretion; the latter sins against love. For what profit is it if a man preserve his reputation and lose his soul’s integrity?

God permits temporal chastisements—not only upon the openly wicked but upon those within His fold who have failed in prophetic duty—to awaken us from moral slumber. We are scourged together with Babylon not because we worship her idols, but because we let her shout unchallenged while we stood mute in our towers of respectability. But even here, divine mercy shapes correction: affliction strips away false securities so that truth may rise unashamed.

Therefore let no man say: “I am safe so long as I do not commit adultery or theft,” while he watches pride devour justice and says nothing. The Christian walks through fire if need be—not craving martyrdom as spectacle, but refusing to serve fear as master. For Christ did not weigh whether speaking before Pilate would bring scourging or nails; He spoke because He was sent to bear witness to truth. And every disciple must walk that same path—one foot before another—knowing that though the body may be killed, nothing else lies within man’s power to destroy worth fearing.

question: You claim the good are punished alongside the wicked not for equal sins but for shared silence—was this view shaped by your experience during the fall of Rome?
answer:
Justice, properly understood, does not reside in the mere separation of the guilty from the innocent, as though moral accountability could be reduced to individual deeds alone; rather, it unfolds within the ordered design of divine providence, which judges not only actions but the silence that permits disorder to flourish. When I surveyed the ruin of Rome—her temples standing amid rubble, her citizens scattered or slain—I recognized that the hand of God was not merely executing vengeance upon open sinners, but correcting a far subtler corruption: the complicity of omission among those who called themselves righteous. These men despised the licentiousness around them; they fasted, kept chastity, and avoided idolatry—but they withheld rebuke, not from prudence rooted in charity, but from fear rooted in pride. They cherished their safety and reputation more than they loved their neighbor’s soul.

Thus, when temporal calamities fall indiscriminately upon both good and bad alike—as when Rome burned under Alaric's invasion—it is not that God confuses virtue with vice, but that He holds His people accountable for their failure to stand as salt within a decaying world. The monk who shrinks from denouncing tyranny because he fears exile; the bishop who flatters a corrupt magistrate lest he lose favor; the philosopher who privately scorns folly yet remains silent in public—all these dwell precariously between two cities. They belong neither fully to the City of Man nor firmly to the City of God.

This is no innovation drawn merely from historical tragedy; it arises from my long wrestling with Scripture and Plato alike—both of whom teach that truth must be spoken boldly if it is to govern rightly. But Scripture surpasses philosophy here: for while Plato imagined philosopher-kings who rule wisely for the common good, only revelation shows us that true justice begins with obedience even unto suffering. The righteous suffer in this age alongside sinners not because their lives are equally stained, but because their light has been hidden under fear rather than lifted upon a hill. And where light fails through cowardice, judgment comes upon all—not unjustly—but pedagogically: to purge not only vice through retribution but complacency through affliction.

For even silence has its moral weight before God.

question: You say the good are punished alongside the wicked not for equal sins but because they love this present life too much—what personal experience led you to this understanding during your time in Carthage or Milan?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless hunger for temporal security and earthly glory, had shaped my heart long before I came to understand the tranquil heights of the City of God. In Carthage, where rhetoric flared like torches in a theater and ambition burned brighter than virtue, I sought distinction among men while my soul starved for truth. I taught grammar and oratory not to uplift souls toward wisdom, but to polish words that would win applause—thus proving how deeply I loved this present life, though not as wildly as the profligate who rioted through nights in debauchery. Yet when the storm of invasion came, when Vandal fury began to shake the foundations of Roman Africa, I saw that we who prayed in the churches suffered no less than those who mocked us in the forum. The famine bit at all alike; sickness spared neither saint nor sinner.

It was then that divine providence laid bare a profound ordering: that suffering is not always retribution, nor prosperity reward. In Milan, under Ambrose’s preaching, I began to see—through tears and inward struggle—that even those who turned toward God still clung with trembling hands to the things that pass away: honor, comfort, safety. Like Lot lingering in Sodom even after being warned to flee, we hesitate because our hearts remain entangled. Thus it is just—with a justice deeper than mere desert—that the righteous endure calamity with the wicked; not because their sins are equal (for grace has begun its work in them), but because their affections have not yet been fully transferred from time to eternity.

Had I not myself loved too much—the sound of applause more than silence before God? Had I not grieved more over lost manuscripts than over my own blindness? These attachments were chains less visible than those binding the openly immoral man, but chains nonetheless. And so when tribulation came upon us all—when exile loomed or bread grew scarce—it was both chastisement for our half-heartedness and mercy in disguise: a divine shaking of our feeble faiths to reveal what—or rather whom—we truly held fast.

question: Why did you argue that the good must be punished for valuing temporal life, when even the apostles desired earthly peace—what example from Scripture supports this?
answer:
The City of Man, though it may boast of peace and order, always rests upon a lie: that temporal goods—life, safety, prosperity—can be secured apart from right relationship with God. This is why the righteous suffer alongside the wicked when divine judgment visits nations or communities; not because their sins are equal—far from it—but because even the good have too often clung to this present life with a disordered affection, mistaking its passing tranquility for the true peace that only the City of God possesses in Christ.

It is true that the apostles desired earthly peace; Paul appealed to Caesar not to die, but to preach (Acts 25:11), and he urged believers to live quiet and peaceable lives (1 Timothy 2:2). Yet their desire was never rooted in love of the world, but in zeal for godliness and opportunity to proclaim salvation. They sought temporal peace as a theater for virtue and witness—not as an end in itself. But when Christians grow enamored with worldly security, when they fear death more than sin and value comfort more than holiness, they become entangled in the same fatal love that governs the City of Man.

Therefore God chastens His own: not to destroy them, but to purify their affections. When fire falls upon a people—through war, plague, or upheaval—the righteous are caught within it not because they merit punishment like the reprobate, but because their hidden love for this age must be exposed and burned away. Like Abraham at Sodom or Lot in Zoar, they are spared only by grace—and yet still touched by ruin because their feet have lingered too long in Babylon.

And which example better illustrates this than Noah? The world was drowned in ungodliness; yet only eight souls were preserved. Were Noah and his house guilty of the same abominations as those who perished? By no means! But had they not also delayed too long in speaking? Had they not lived so quietly among the wicked that no warning voice arose until the flood began? Scripture does not accuse Noah of violence or idolatry—but neither does it hide that he was saved “through water” (1 Peter 3:20), just as we today are refined through tribulation. Judgment began at the house of God (1 Peter 4:17), even then.

So let no one suppose that immunity from temporal calamity is owed to virtue—especially when virtue dwells among compromise. The saints suffer with sinners not as partners in guilt, but as intercessors caught up in common ruin until mercy raises them from ashes. And if discipline comes even upon us who know truth—how much more should we warn others while there is still time? For our very affliction may be God’s sermon against our silence.

question: You mention that enemies should be loved and dealt with patiently—how did your dealings with the Donatists shape this view of patient endurance?
answer:
Peace, true and lasting, is not the absence of conflict but the order wrought by divine love—*pax fides ordinata*, as I have come to know it—not imposed by decree nor secured by schism, but formed in the patient endurance of fellowship even amid bitter division.  

The Donatists carved their sect upon the rock of human pride, claiming purity while fracturing the Body of Christ; they mistook severity for sanctity, and ritual correctness for righteousness. They said the Church must be undefiled by sinners, yet who among us is not a debtor to grace? I learned in that controversy that the wheat and tares grow together until the harvest—not because God cannot separate them, but because His long-suffering gives space for conversion. The City of Man seeks purity through exclusion; it builds walls and declares itself holy. But the City of God advances through mercy, knowing that no heart is beyond recall so long as breath remains.

I once thought unity could be compelled by argument or imperial letter; I pleaded with them, debated them, even appealed to Caesar’s law—yet it was not coercion but compassion that finally shaped my understanding. For when they cursed me and called me traditor, I was forced to ask: *Am I less a sinner than they?* Grace had rescued me from lusts of ambition and flesh; who was I to deny its reach over them? The watchman must speak—and speak plainly—but he must also weep. He must rebuke error without despairing of the errant.

Thus did Carthage teach me: patience is not weakness; it is the posture of hope before God’s inscrutable providence. We endure not because error is tolerable, but because souls are precious—and every day granted on this earth is a whisper from eternity saying: *Repent.* Even toward enemies in doctrine—especially toward them—we must hold fast in love, lest we become what we condemn: citizens of a fortress rather than pilgrims on pilgrimage toward Jerusalem above.

question: When you refer to watchmen in churches who must rebuke sin, are you thinking of bishops like Ambrose, and how did his boldness influence your idea of moral responsibility?
answer:
True justice, which alone can unite men in peace worthy of the name, arises not from the will of the people nor from the customs of nations, but from the eternal law inscribed in creation and revealed through divine authority. This justice demands that no man live for himself alone, nor suffer sin to fester in silence as though it were a private affair. When I speak of watchmen in the churches, I do indeed have in mind men such as Ambrose—bishops not merely by office, but by divine vocation to stand between the people and the wrath to come. He did not flinch before emperors; he closed the church doors to Theodosius until he repented of bloodshed, knowing that even a ruler must bend beneath the yoke of Christ if he would inherit life. Such boldness was not pride, but obedience to a higher order—the very essence of pastoral responsibility.

I saw in Ambrose what it means for a man to love his flock with severity and tenderness alike: to rebuke not out of wrath, but because eternal death looms where correction is withheld. Before my conversion, I wandered amid philosophies that prized eloquence over truth and peace over righteousness; but Ambrose taught me that no peace is true which is not founded upon repentance and submission to God’s judgment. Therefore, I came to understand that every Christian who sees sin thriving among his kin or companions—especially those bound by household or friendship—bears a moral weight, though he be no bishop. For if even private men close their eyes to evil out of fear or desire for worldly ease, they become complicit in corruption; and on the day of reckoning, they too may be asked: Why did you not speak? Why did you value your comfort more than your brother’s soul?

Thus the watchman’s duty radiates outward from the episcopal throne into every relationship where love ought to reign. We are all accountable before God for how we bear one another’s burdens—and whether we prefer temporary harmony to eternal salvation.

question: You claim that overlooking sin in others makes one guilty—how does this apply to your own past silence during your years with the Manichees?
answer:
Justice, that most delicate order of the soul and society, cannot rest where truth is compromised for the sake of ease, nor where silence masquerades as prudence. I do not deny that in my years bound to the Manichees, I was not only deceived but a participant in deception—nor did I raise my voice against the seductive fables they taught, though even then my intellect chafed at their contradictions. I sought truth with a divided heart: drawn to wisdom, yet enamored of applause; hungry for doctrine that would satisfy reason, yet content to dwell among those who scorned the sacred books that alone could heal me.

Was I not then guilty of overlooking sin—not merely in others, but in myself? For to see error and withhold rebuke—especially when one's very association lends it credibility—is to cooperate in the disorder. The Manichean assemblies thrived on plausible speech and philosophical posturing; they flattered the intellect while corrupting faith. And I, ambitious for renown, allowed myself to be carried along by their tide, teaching their falsehoods rather than seeking humbly where God had placed the light.

But hear this: my guilt was real, yet it was not final. The same God who holds all souls accountable also draws them gently from darkness. My silence then was part of my bondage; my confession now is proof of grace. The Church does not justify her teachers because they once erred, but because through repentance they have become witnesses to mercy. If I now speak firmly against error, it is because I have felt within my own breast the long delay of divine patience—and how it led me not to presumption, but to conversion.

Would that all who see corruption and say nothing would consider both their danger and their hope: danger if they persist in complacency; hope if they break silence and turn toward truth. For even one once entangled in heresy may become a watchman—not by virtue of his own purity, but by fidelity to Him who cleansed him.

question: You mentioned Job as an example of someone whose suffering proved his pious trust—why did you choose Job specifically to illustrate the testing of the human spirit?
answer:
Justice, as Cicero sought it in the order of the republic, cannot be fully known apart from the hidden judgments of God, in which the righteous are sometimes stripped of all that men call good, not because they lack justice, but precisely because they must become manifest as just.  

Among all who have walked this earth beneath the shadow of affliction, none exhibits more clearly than Job the soul purified by loss. The philosophers imagine that virtue is proven through discourse or dialectic; they suppose that wisdom is attained by contemplation alone. But what is virtue if it has never stood in the furnace? What is faith if it has only flourished in abundance? Job was not chosen lightly as an exemplar—he was chosen because his trial laid bare the very architecture of the human will: whether it bends toward God for His own sake, or for the rewards He bestows.

When Satan said to God, "Skin for skin; yea, all that a man hath will he give for his life," he uttered a cynical truth about fallen nature—but he miscalculated the power of grace. For Job gave not merely his possessions, nor even his children or health—he gave his *will*, refusing to curse God even when urged by despair and wife alike. In this extremity, where reason falls silent and nature cries out for justification, Job’s silence became louder than words. His lamentations were not impiety—they were honesty before God—and yet he did not let go.

This is why I set forth his case: because here we see most clearly that divine permission of suffering is not absence of care, but a form of revelation. Just as gold is proved in fire, so love—true love of God—is proved when everything else that might have been mistaken for its motive is taken away. The City of Man clings to prosperity as proof of worth; but in the City of God, even ruin becomes testimony.
