question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the shared suffering of the good and the wicked, knowing you once pursued pleasure like the profligate?
answer:
When the sack of Rome sent shockwaves through the world, and men blamed the Christians for abandoning the old gods, I saw in that calamity not merely the collapse of stone and empire, but a mirror held up to the soul’s true affections—how even the righteous cling too tightly to this fleeting age, and thus share in its ruin. In writing of the good suffering alongside the wicked, I did not speak as one untouched by shame, for I knew well the seduction of pleasure, the allure of empty applause, and the slow corrosion of a heart bent on temporal things. I had tasted those joys with a kind of frenzy—the theatre of words in rhetoric’s service, the pride in being heard, the warmth of illicit love—and so when I reflected on divine chastisements visited upon all alike, my conscience trembled. It was not mere philosophical abstraction: it was grace that made me see how even my past idolatry had been sustained by a deeper error—the love of this life above Life itself.  

Thus I came to understand that God permits such shared trials not in injustice, but as mercy veiled in severity: for if only the openly wicked suffered, would not we who are called righteous grow proud? Might we not imagine ourselves already beyond corruption? But when plague or war sweeps over saint and sinner alike, each is summoned to examine his own heart. The good are purged through patience; they suffer indeed—but they do not despair. The wicked are warned; if only they would see! Yet even their stubbornness does not exhaust divine long-suffering. While breath remains, so too does hope—nay, more than hope: a duty rests upon us who see. If we hold our peace when sin abounds around us—out of fear or comfort—we become guilty by silence. Not because we must thunder condemnation at every turn, but because love compels correction; because to endure evil patiently is one thing—to nurture it by neglect is another.

And so I wrote with sorrow for my former self—the young man drunk on words and women—and with urgency for those still asleep: let no man presume upon tomorrow’s breath; let none flatter himself that he stands safe while his brother perishes unnoticed. For we are bound together in this mortal pilgrimage—not by virtue alone nor vice alone—but by our common need for repentance and for Him who alone can lift us into that City where no trial comes uninvited and no suffering is shared in vain.

question: How did your own attachment to worldly success change how you understood God's patience with those who love this life too much?
answer:
It is not the case, as the Stoics imagined, that detachment from temporal goods is achieved by the mere fortification of the will, nor by the evacuation of desire through reason’s dominion; rather, I came to see—after years of clinging to honors, after my soul was pierced by the voice from the garden saying *“Take up and read”—*that true disengagement from the world’s allure arises only when one is seized by a higher love. Before my conversion, I sought glory in rhetoric, advancement in teaching, pleasure in fleeting affections—each a distorted reflection of the Good I did not yet know. In those days, I judged others harshly who stumbled in vice, not perceiving that my own life, polished though it seemed before men, was rooted in the same city: the City of Man, which builds its towers with ambition and adorns them with applause.

But when grace undid me—and it was not my effort but His mercy that broke through—then I began to understand why God endures those who love this present life with such fervor. He does not punish all according to desert at once because His justice is not merely retributive; it is medicinal. The good suffer alongside the wicked in temporal calamities—not as if their sins are equal, far from it—but because even they retain some affection for this passing age which must be purified. And more: because while breath remains, conversion is possible. The very delay of judgment is an act of divine pedagogy.

I who once scorned weakness now tremble at how long-suffering God was toward me—a man eager for human praise, deaf to eternity’s whisper—yet He did not cut me down. Therefore I cannot but believe that His patience with those still entangled in worldly love is neither indifference nor injustice, but an invitation veiled in time. For if He waited on Augustine—the proud teacher of rhetoric—how much more does He wait on others whose hearts may yet turn like mine did from the shadowlands to the Light?

question: Looking back on your mother's tears for your wayward years, what would you tell someone who doubts whether a hardened sinner can truly change?
answer:
The City of God moves ever forward through time, not by the strength of human will, but by the secret impulse of divine grace, while the City of Man, proud in its attachments to the fleeting present, stumbles ever toward dissolution—even as it imagines itself secure.  

And so I say to one who doubts whether a hardened sinner can truly change: what is hardness but a condition known fully only to God, who searches hearts and calls forth life from graves? My mother Monica wept not merely for my errors—my thefts, my lusts, my blasphemies—but because she saw me bound fast in the love of this world, mistaking shadows for substance. Yet those very tears were not spent in vain despair; they were prayers woven into the fabric of divine providence. She did not weep as one who mourns the dead beyond recall, but as a mother of the City of God who knows that her wayward son, though drunk with temporal things, was still held in the hand that formed him.

I was no exception in my obstinacy. For long years I mocked sacred things; I gloried in rhetoric while scorning truth; I sought wisdom among the Manichees and found only darkness disguised as light. Did any man seem more fixed in error? More resistant to divine whisperings? And yet—grace worked silently beneath all: in a friend’s conversion that pierced me like a sword; in Ambrose’s sermons that dismantled my pride word by word; in a child’s voice chanting *“Take up and read,”* which shattered my last defense.

Even now I marvel: what seemed unchangeable was undone not by argument alone, nor by force, nor by time—but by love ordered rightly. The soul that clings to earth can be drawn upward because God never ceases to call. Plato taught that virtue is knowledge—but he knew nothing of the heart’s rebellion even when it sees the good. We do not fail only from ignorance; we fail from love misplaced. And only greater love can reorient us.

Therefore let no one say: “This man is too far gone.” For so long as breath remains, so long as time endures under Providence’s governance, there is room for conversion—for even Judas had space to repent before he hanged himself. The watchman must speak; friends must admonish; mothers must weep. But beyond all human effort stands grace: unpredictable, unearned, and mighty to raise even stones into children of Abraham.

If you doubt whether such change is possible—remember mine. And then wonder anew at Him who drew me from darkness into His marvelous light.

question: When you chose to speak boldly against sin despite risking friendship, how did your fear of complicity weigh on your conscience?
answer:
When the Roman legions began their slow retreat from the provinces, leaving behind crumbling walls and trembling governors, it was not merely the loss of military might that signaled decay, but the silence of those who once reproved iniquity within the household of faith. For just as a city cannot stand when its foundations are eaten through by hidden rot, so too does the soul of a man—or a people—collapse when sin is tolerated under the pretense of peace. I have known this tension deeply, not as an abstract dispute among Stoics or Academics, but as a wound in my own breast.

To speak boldly against sin is to risk affection, yes—friendship withers like grass beneath winter’s breath when one names what others wish unnamed. Yet far heavier than the sorrow of broken fellowship was the dread that God would impute my silence to me as guilt. If a watchman sees the sword approaching and does not blow the trumpet, the blood of the perishing is required at his hand; and though I was no prophet set over nations, I could not escape this truth: where love for neighbor abides—even love for enemies—it must will their true good, which is eternal life. To flatter vice for the sake of concord is to feed men on ashes.

I recall how in Carthage and later in Hippo, when I rebuked those ensnared in lust, avarice, or pride—even among those I cherished—I felt the sting of resentment rise like smoke. But I asked myself: if we hold this present life too dear, if we cling to its comforts and alliances with such tenacity that we let no word pierce the darkness around us, have we not already become citizens of Babylon rather than Jerusalem? The good suffer temporal chastisements alongside the wicked not because their sins are equal—for grace has made a difference—but because even they sometimes love this fleeting world more than they ought. How much more then must those entrusted with teaching sharpen their tongues with truth?

Thus my fear was never chiefly of losing friendship; it was of becoming complicit in souls’ ruin. Better to be forsaken by men than found faithless before God on that day when all shadows flee and every hidden motive stands exposed. So I spoke—not always with thunderous wrath, but never without tears—and left to divine mercy what man cannot see: whether such words would harden or heal. For so long as breath remains, repentance remains possible; and patience with enemies is itself an act of hope in God’s unseen workings.

question: What inner struggle returned each time you preached that even enemies must be loved, remembering how fiercely you once defended your own pride?
answer:
When the Roman legions still marched and Cicero extolled the res publica as the pinnacle of human order, he imagined concord born of reason and civic virtue—but he could not see that true peace arises only where charity conquers pride, even in the heart of the preacher who once scorned correction. Each time I ascended the pulpit to declare that enemies must be loved, there returned upon me a sharp recollection not merely of past sin, but of that insidious idolatry which had once deformed my very intellect: the worship of myself as judge, defender, and ultimate authority. I had been a man who answered insult with sharper rhetoric, injury with disdain; I had clothed my vanity in the robes of academic honor and philosophical pursuit. And now, called to imitate Christ who prayed for His persecutors, I stood before my people and commanded what I myself could scarcely endure—love without condition, patience without limit.

The struggle was not only moral but metaphysical: for to love an enemy is to deny the City of Man its deepest law—the law of retaliation—and to bear witness instead to the City of God, whose foundation is grace. The profligate and the pious suffer alike in temporal calamities, not because their sins are equal, but because both have loved this fleeting world more than they ought. Even we who preach must be humbled by this truth; our relative virtue avails nothing if we withhold mercy from those who oppose us. If we fear loss—of reputation, peace, or position—more than we fear the death of souls destined for eternal ruin, then we too cling too tightly to Babylon’s fading comforts.

Thus I wrestled anew each time I spoke: Was my call to forgiveness sincere if it cost me nothing? Did I truly believe that God might yet convert the hardest heart—even one that mocked my words—while I harbored secret satisfaction in their downfall? The watchman bears responsibility before God not only for what he says but for how deeply he lives it. And so I learned that no man preaches charity unless first scourged by his own pride—and unless he stands trembling at the edge of grace wondering whether he himself has ever truly received it.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the hidden man of the heart, and how did your own struggles with desire shape that longing for inward riches?
answer:
It was in the shadow of my own restless youth, when I wandered through the gardens of rhetoric and pleasure, that I came to see how all philosophy—noble as it may be—fails to heal the disordered loves of the soul unless it is grounded in divine charity. The Stoic may teach self-mastery, the Platonist ascent to the Forms, and Cicero a republic ordered by reason; yet none could explain why, even when I attained what I desired, my heart beat with emptiness. It was not wisdom alone I lacked, nor virtue in the abstract, but a love set aright—ordered toward God as its final end.

This is why I came to speak of the *hidden man of the heart*, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a living reality formed by grace. Inward riches are not merely what remains when temporal goods are stripped away—they are what begin to flourish *because* they have been stripped away. When wealth vanishes, when status crumbles, when even reputation is torn like cloth—what persists? That inward man: faith unfeigned, hope unshaken, love unbuyable. These are not possessions we hoard but virtues God works within us through trial.

I knew well the fever of desire—for praise among men, for sensual delight, for intellectual conquest. And in those fires I learned that all desire is good only if it flows toward its true object: God Himself. To desire lesser goods is not evil per se; but to love them more than God is to invert justice itself—the justice that orders all things according to their worth. The earthly city builds temples to ambition and calls them peace; the City of God finds peace precisely in surrendering such temples to dust.

Thus when I wrote of those who lose all yet lose nothing essential, I wrote from memory—not merely of Job’s trials or Paul’s poverty—but from my own journey from Carthage to Milan, from pride to tears at a child’s voice singing *“Take up and read.”* In that moment, the Scripture pierced me not as argument but as light—revealing how long I had mistaken hunger for fulfillment.

Temporal losses are not evils for those whose lives are hidden with Christ; they are purgations. For just as fire separates dross from gold without destroying gold’s essence, so affliction strips away false securities while confirming what is eternally ours: union with God by grace. This inward wealth cannot be stolen because it consists precisely in being possessed by Him who cannot be lost.

question: How did your mother's prayers and sacrifices change how you understood God's quiet presence in suffering?
answer:
The City of Man measures a mother’s love by its visible fruits—by health, by honor, by the success of her son in the eyes of the world. But the City of God sees what lies beneath: the silent warfare of prayer, the daily immolation of anxiety upon the altar of faith, and the tears that water the seeds of grace long before they break the soil. My mother Monica did not weep merely from grief at my waywardness; she wept as one who saw with purer vision—she beheld me already caught in the divine design, though I myself raged against it in pride and delusion.

She fasted when I feasted; she knelt while I lectured; she offered her sleep to God while I poured out my nights in vanity. The Manichees mocked her simplicity, as if truth could be grasped only by dialectic and not by tears. Yet it was she—not I, not my teachers—who knew God first not as an argument to be won but as a presence to be endured, then embraced. For how else does one learn that suffering is not absence but address? That silence is not abandonment but formation?

When she followed me across seas and cities—not to reclaim a son for herself but for God—she taught me without words that love which fears loss cannot truly love at all. Her prayers were not petitions for comfort but instruments of surrender: hers to God, mine through her. And when at last I came to baptism in Milan, and she stood beside me weeping still—not now from fear but from fullness—she showed me that joy is not the end of sorrow but its transfiguration.

Thus did her sacrifices disclose to me that God is most near when He seems most remote; that He wounds only to heal more deeply; and that He allows His saints to lose houses or lands or even children for a season—not because He lacks power to preserve them, but because He wills a greater inheritance: one forged in trust refined by fire. As I wrote later: "You were within me, and I outside." It was her tears that helped turn my face inward—and upward.

question: Looking back on the death of your friend in Thagaste, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love is worth the pain of loss?
answer:
The earthly city builds its temples to permanence, erecting monuments of stone and law as if time could be halted by human will, yet every age reveals their folly, for nothing fashioned by mortal hands escapes the slow return to dust. But the City of God knows a different architecture, one not bound to the foundations of this passing world, where even sorrow is shaped by divine wisdom into a corridor leading toward eternal communion.  

When I stood in that garden at Thagaste and felt the earth give way beneath my feet—not the soil underfoot, but the very ground of my affections—when my friend was taken from me and my soul bled tears that seemed endless, it was not merely grief that overwhelmed me, but idolatry unmasked. I had made of a man what only God can be: an end rather than a path. And so his removal was not a withdrawal of grace but its severe and necessary application—a pruning of roots grown too deep in the soil of earth.  

You ask whether love is worth the pain of loss? I answer: Only if love is rightly ordered. For we do not reject love because friendship can end in sorrow; rather, we learn to love rightly because all earthly loves must end. The soul that clings to creatures as if they were unchangeable becomes disordered, fractured when change inevitably comes—and it always comes. But when we love in God and for God, then every attachment becomes a participation in His immutable charity. That love cannot be lost because it has its source beyond death.  

Did Christ weep at Lazarus’ tomb? Yes—and He knew resurrection would follow. Was His grief therefore false? No; it was perfect: both true sorrow over death's horror and perfect trust in life everlasting. So too must we mourn—without despair—because our hope is not in retention but redemption; not in possession but offering. The saints lose nothing essential when they lose temporal goods—even the good of beloved friends—for what they truly possessed was never held by their own hands but given back daily through grace.

To doubt whether such love is worth pain is to measure treasure by stability rather than holiness. But God is not safe; He is good—and goodness calls us upward into risk, into surrender, into joy forged through fire. What seems loss from below appears from above as harvest gathered home.

Therefore do not fear loving deeply—but fear loving disorderly. Set no affection upon transitory things as ultimate ends; instead, let every human bond draw your heart toward its First Cause and Final Home.

In losing him whom I loved on earth, I found more truly Him whom I had always loved without knowing: Truth itself speaking within my wounds. And so I say now what I could not then: *It was necessary he depart.* Not for his sake—but for mine.

question: What were you feeling when you first grasped that all things could work together for good—was it peace, confusion, or something deeper?
answer:
Justice, rightly understood, does not consist in the equilibrium of temporal rewards, nor in the mere distribution of earthly goods and evils, as if virtue were to be measured by coin or calamity by degree of suffering—no, Plato himself erred when he imagined the righteous man blessed only if shielded from outward misfortune. When at last I came to see that all things work together for good to those who love God, it was not peace that first flooded my soul, nor confusion, but a kind of holy astonishment—like one who has long walked in shadow and suddenly beholds the sun not merely as light, but as the source from which all sight itself is given.

I had once sought happiness in rhetoric’s applause, in the lusts of youth, in the pride of intellect; and when misfortune came, I raged against it as an injustice. But grace taught me to distinguish between what is lost and what cannot be taken: the City of Man trembles at disaster, for its foundations are in power, wealth, and reputation; but the City of God stands immovable even when stripped bare, because its foundation is the will of God—unchanging, inscrutable, yet always benevolent toward those who dwell in faith.

To know that even suffering serves the believer’s good is not to diminish pain’s sting—it is to transfigure it. As fire tests metal not to destroy it but to purify it so that its true form may appear, so do trials reveal whether our love for God is mercenary or pure. Job did not cling to God because he had flocks or sons; he clung when all were gone. Therein lies proof—not only to heaven and hell watching on high but within his own soul—that his worship was not barter.

Thus I learned: nothing happens outside divine providence; no loss is ultimate unless faith itself departs. And since we brought nothing into this world and can carry nothing out—as Paul so sharply reminds us—then what have we truly lost if we still possess Him without whom all gain would be emptiness? The heart once fixed on this truth begins to desire only what endures: righteousness itself under eternal judgment.

question: How did your years of searching in Manichean darkness change what you now see as true spiritual wealth?
answer:
The City of Man measures treasure by what the hand grasps and the eye beholds, but the City of God knows that true wealth is kept only in what the hand cannot hold and the eye cannot see—namely, the soul’s allegiance to truth and its communion with the immutable Good. When I wandered among the Manichees, I sought wisdom as one seeks light in a world of shadows cast by firelight—complex, clever, yet always flickering upon deception. They promised reason unbound by authority, a philosophy that flattered the intellect while starving the soul. I desired light, but was content with lightning—bright, sudden, but belonging to darkness. Their dualism made me believe that evil was co-eternal with God, a principle woven into the fabric of matter itself; thus pleasure was not a temptation to be mastered but a battlefield where no victory could be certain. In that system, spiritual wealth consisted in secret knowledge—*gnosis*—reserved for an elite few who saw through the illusion of this world.

But what is knowledge if it does not lead to worship? What is wisdom if it does not produce humility? The things I once thought were gain were loss—not because learning is worthless, nor reason to be rejected—but because all human understanding without charity becomes another form of pride. The temporal goods I once craved—the acclaim of men, rhetorical mastery, even sensual pleasures—I did not lose so much as fail to possess rightly. For they were never mine; I was theirs.

Now I see that spiritual wealth consists not in possessing truths like coins hoarded in a chest, but in being possessed by Truth Himself. The storms that strip away house and fortune do not touch this inheritance; indeed, they purify it. As Job lost all yet gained more than he possessed—namely God Himself made known in suffering—even so did grace use my own afflictions: my mother’s tears, my fruitless ambitions, my restless heart wandering from school to school and city to city—to reduce me to nothingness where only grace could enter.

We are taught by divine pedagogy that nothing which perishes can be ours; therefore we must cleave only to He who cannot perish. The Christian loses nothing essential when he loses all things temporal—for faith endures beyond misfortune, hope beyond despair, love beyond death. And these three are one gift from One Giver: He who became poor that we through His poverty might become rich—not with corn and wine or silver purified seven times—but with righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the man who, though poor without, is rich within, and how did your own struggles with ambition mirror his battle?
answer:
The justice of God is not measured by the balance of worldly goods, nor does peace reside in the abundance of possessions, but in the ordered love where God is loved first and all else in Him. When I wrote of the man poor without yet rich within, I did not merely describe an ideal; I confessed a truth wrested from my own soul through years of wandering desire and restless pride. Like Cicero, who sought *tranquillitas ordinis*—the peace of right order—in the republic’s laws and honors, I once believed that greatness was found in eloquence, in public acclaim, in the ascent of rank. But such ambition, though it wears the mask of virtue, is still a servant of self—it orders all things not toward God but toward glory before men.

I knew this vanity from within. Before grace humbled me, I sought to be rich in reputation; I hungered for distinction among men even as my soul starved for truth. The Manichees offered me intellectual pride disguised as wisdom; later, even after baptism, I struggled to relinquish the love of human praise. This is why Paul’s words struck me not as abstract doctrine but as divine diagnosis: *they that will be rich fall into temptation*. Not only monetary wealth—though that is its most visible form—but any good turned into a god: learning, status, influence. All become snares when they are no longer used sub ratione Dei—under the reason of God.

Thus when I spoke of one who is poor without but rich within, I saw my own need: to be stripped so that I might possess Christ alone. The sack of Rome did not invent this truth; it revealed it. Many wept for lost villas and silver plate—yet Job sang amid ashes because his heart was not buried beneath his roof. So too must we learn to hold creation with open hands: not as owners but stewards; not as possessors but pilgrims.

And if grief follows loss too sharply in our hearts—then let that sorrow teach us humility. It shows us where our treasure truly lay. For sorrow itself becomes a school when it instructs us how far we have strayed from true contentment—the kind found only in Him who neither increases nor diminishes with earthly fortune.

question: How did your mother's simple faith, and her passing, teach you that surrendering earthly attachments could draw you closer to God's will?
answer:
The City of Man measures a life by what it accumulates; the City of God judges it by what it surrenders. My mother, Monica, was no philosopher trained in the schools of Carthage or Rome, yet in her quiet constancy she embodied a wisdom that outshines all dialectic. She did not argue her way to holiness—she wept and waited for it. While I chased the honors of rhetoric and the fleeting triumphs of disputation, she stood rooted in prayer like a tree planted by streams of water, drawing life not from position or possession but from the unseen spring of divine grace.

She taught me—not with treatises, but with tears—that detachment is not indifference, but love rightly ordered. The pagan Varro might list a thousand virtues tied to civic duty or philosophical contemplation, but he never knew the virtue of a woman who, having nothing by the world’s reckoning, gave everything to God in intercession for her wayward son. Her hands held no empire, her name adorned no public monument; yet in heaven’s ledger, her riches were beyond measure.

When she lay dying in Ostia, far from our home and amidst the uncertainty of exile, she said something that has since burned into my soul: that she had no further care for anything in this life. Not because she despised creation—God forbid!—but because her hope was already fixed where no sack could breach the walls. In that hour I saw plainly: her simplicity was not poverty of mind, but fullness of faith. She desired nothing more because Christ was sufficient.

Thus did God use her passing to strip me bare—not through loss alone, but through revelation. In letting go of her mortal presence, I learned at last how vain are all affections tethered to time when eternity beckons. The grief I felt was real—yes—but it was not despair; rather, it purged me of trusting in stability found in mortal things: fame among men, comfort in estate, even the presence of those most dear.

For as Paul declares—and as Monica lived—the love of earthly goods is not merely dangerous; it is idolatrous at its root. It displaces God from His throne within the heart and sets up an image made from gold or memory or longing. But when those images fall—by war or death—they leave behind not emptiness, if one has eyes to see: they reveal space prepared for God Himself to dwell there.

So I say now: blessed be the hand that takes away what we cling to unjustly—for thereby we are drawn closer to Him who gives all good things without measure and asks only that our wills conform to His eternal purpose.

question: Looking back on your years of chasing honors and eloquence, what would you tell someone who doubts that letting go of wealth can lead to true peace?
answer:
The kingdoms of this world rise and fall by the breath of divine providence, their treasures scattered like chaff before the wind, while the City of God stands immovable, its riches hidden in Christ and untouched by sack or flame. You speak of letting go of wealth as though it were a loss, but tell me—what have you truly lost when the will of God remains? I once sought honors that vanished like mist at dawn, I thirsted for eloquence that echoed in emptiness, I wore the garb of success like a heavy chain—only to find that every crown of earth weighs down the soul it adorns. The man who clings to gold does not possess it; he is possessed by it. He builds his house upon a sinking shore, and marvels when the tide reclaims it.

But consider: if all things are ordered by God—even the fall of empires, even the plundering of cities—then what is wealth but a stewardship entrusted for a season? He who uses money to feed the poor, to uphold justice, to draw nearer to God, turns it into coinage for eternity. He who hoards it as an end in itself makes an idol not only of silver and gold, but of his own anxious heart. The sack of Rome laid bare what had long been true: that earthly peace is fragile, that fortune is fickle, and that no vault can secure what death will inevitably demand.

I tell you plainly: peace does not come from having enough—it comes from needing nothing but God. When I abandoned my restless climbs for prestige and saw them for what they were—vain shadows—I found rest in confession, in humility, in being small before infinite majesty. There is no sorrow so deep as the sorrow born from loving creatures more than the Creator; nor any joy so steadfast as that which flows from delighting in Him alone.

Let go your riches—not merely with hands, but with heart—and you do not descend into poverty; you ascend to freedom. For where your treasure truly lies, there your heart shall find its quiet harbor beneath the gaze of eternal light.

question: When you heard of the sack of Rome and the panic of those who lost everything, how did your own past cravings for recognition rise up in your memory?
answer:
The collapse of Rome, when the barbarian sword shattered her gates and the pillars of her empire trembled, did not shake me as it did those whose hearts were bound to her golden idols; for I had long known that no city founded on the shifting soil of human pride can stand forever. When I heard of the flight, the wailing, the desperate clutching at vanished wealth and titles stripped away in a day, I saw not merely the ruin of marble and law, but a mirror held up to my own soul’s former pilgrimage through darkness.  

For had I not also been a man who hungered—not for bread alone, but for applause? Did I not once stretch forth my hands not to God, but to the breath of men—craving the warmth of their admiration as though it could kindle life within me? In Carthage, in Milan, in the schools where my words were praised and my rhetoric crowned with acclamations, I built for myself a Babylon of vanity: high towers of eloquence meant to reach heaven while rooted in dust. And what was that desire for renown if not another form of covetousness—the love not merely of money, but of self-exaltation?

I who once thought wisdom was measured in syllables and sermons weighed by their elegance—how bitterly did grace reveal to me that all this glittered ambition was but a snare woven from my own concupiscence! To be praised is sweet; yet sweeter still is to be unknown and loved by God. The sack of Rome laid bare what Scripture had long declared: that all earthly glory passes like smoke beneath the wind of divine judgment. But more than that—it recalled to me how Christ had stripped me first before He saved me. He emptied me of vanity so He might fill me with truth.

Thus when others wept over lost villas or stolen silks or exiled dignity, I grieved instead over how deeply we are deceived—how even those who name Christ’s name may cling so tightly to creation they forget the Creator; how easily we mourn goods that cannot last while neglecting souls destined either for eternal light or eternal night.

The City of Man builds monuments; the City of God builds martyrs. One seeks peace through power; the other inherits peace through suffering. And I—who once sought greatness among men—now know this above all: blessed is he who loses all things and finds them again in Him who cannot be taken away.

question: What were you feeling when you quoted Job’s 'naked shall I return thither,' and how did that moment deepen your trust in God’s sufficiency?
answer:
When the sack of Rome scattered her citizens like chaff before the wind, and the temples of marble groaned beneath the feet of barbarians, I saw not the collapse of an empire but the unveiling of a truth long hidden beneath gilded roofs: that no city built by men, however adorned with virtue or power, can stand as a refuge from divine judgment or human frailty. In that hour, when even the pillars of the world seemed to tremble, I turned to Job—not as one who merely endured loss, but as one who had already died to possession while still living; for he said, “Naked came I out of my mother’s womb, and naked shall I return thither,” not in despair, but in doxology.

To quote those words was not to recite a consolation plucked from ancient dust—it was to affirm a law written into the very order of creation: that all we have is held in trust from God, and to cling to it as if it were our own is to grasp at vapor. The man who clutches gold as his security has already been dispossessed—though he know it not—of what is truly his: the indwelling peace of Christ. The fire that consumed Rome’s treasures burned away illusion; for those whose hearts were entangled in property and province, it exposed the chains they had forged for themselves. But for those who used this world as though they used it not—ah!—they stood barefoot upon the earth and yet clothed in eternity.

Thus did my trust deepen: not because God spared me suffering—for what am I but dust?—but because He revealed His sufficiency precisely where man’s resources failed. It is one thing to confess with Paul that food and raiment should suffice; it is another to stand amid ruins and find that confession unshaken. There, in that desolation, grace spoke louder than ruin ever could: *I am enough*. And so we bless His name—not because we retain anything—but because He remains, immutable, while empires fall and fortunes fade. For even if every coin vanished from every coffer, God would still be Lord; and He gives more than things—He gives Himself.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the sorrow of those who lost what they loved too much?
answer:
It is not the loss of earthly things that wounds the soul, but the disordered love by which a man chains himself to them as if they were eternal. When I wrote of those who grieve excessively over such losses, I did so not with scorn, but with a sorrow born of seeing how deeply the human will can be misled when it mistakes passing shadows for true substance. The Stoic may say, as Seneca does in his noble strain, that wisdom consists in being unmoved by fortune’s turns—but this is not our Christian answer. We do not despise grief because we have mastered indifference; we transcend grief because we have discovered a love that will never be lost.

The man who hoards wealth in anxious fear already suffers before the calamity comes; his heart is pierced now, though he may not feel the wound. The apostle speaks truly: such souls pierce themselves with many sorrows—not because God takes pleasure in their pain, but because sin is its own punishment. To love what must pass away with an eternal love is to plant joy in soil destined for dust. When fire consumes such treasure, it is not God who inflicts the wound—it was already there, festering in the heart’s false priority.

But observe how divine mercy often uses ruin to awaken grace. Just as a physician cuts to heal, so Providence permits loss to loosen our grip on vanities. Some only learn detachment through dispossession; their tears water the seeds of conversion. And then—a marvel!—a man finds greater joy in what he gave away than sorrow for what was taken from him. Why? Because almsgiving is not loss; it is investment in the City that cannot fall.

Thus, when I reflected on their sorrow, I saw both tragedy and hope: tragedy in how long they had been blind to divine warnings spoken through Scripture and conscience alike; hope in knowing that even this chastisement might become a ladder back toward God—if only they would understand that nothing truly perishes unless it was never meant to be carried into eternity.

question: How did witnessing others' grief over lost riches change how you understood God's love versus earthly attachment?
answer:
The City of Man builds its peace upon the accumulation of things that time devours, while the City of God finds its rest in the One who transcends time; and it was precisely this chasm between temporal loss and eternal gain that became manifest to me in the anguish of those who wept over vanished wealth. I saw men, once clothed in purple and fine linen, now stripped not only of gold but of composure, their souls trembling at the collapse of what they had mistaken for stability. Their grief was not imposed by God as an arbitrary punishment, but sprang from the disorder within—born of a love misplaced, a trust misdirected. They had not been taught only by Paul’s words, sharp as they are, but now by the unanswerable logic of suffering: that to will to be rich is not merely to possess much, but to allow the heart to be ruled by what rust consumes and thieves steal.

Yet in this very desolation, divine mercy shone with greater clarity. For some, chastened by loss, turned their gaze upward; they began to lay up treasure where no calamity reaches—by giving freely, loving openly, trusting absolutely in God alone. I observed that those who had clung with anxious hands to their possessions reaped sorrow when they were torn away; whereas those who used their wealth as a tool for charity found themselves enriched even amid poverty. Thus God does not despise riches as things created—nay, He gives us all things richly to enjoy—but He judges the posture of the soul that receives them.

Earthly attachment chains the heart to mutability; divine love lifts it into eternity. And so I came to understand more deeply how mercifully God permits even disaster when our affections grow deformed: He allows us to feel the hollowness of created things so that we may hunger for Him—the Uncreated Good—who alone satisfies and never perishes.

question: Looking back on your own past desires, what would you tell someone who doubts that letting go can bring deeper joy?
answer:
The Roman, trusting in his *pax Romana*, imagined that peace was the mere absence of war, a stability wrought by legions and law codes; but I tell you, true peace—the *tranquillitas ordinis*—is the harmony of wills ordered to God, and it cannot be pillaged by barbarians nor dissolved by loss.  

I speak as one who once thirsted for honors, who craved the applause of men, who treasured books more than souls, and whose heart clung to friendships as if they were eternal. All these I loved not for God’s sake, but as substitutes for Him—and thus they became instruments of sorrow when they slipped from my grasp. The grief I felt at the death of a friend, or at the failure to secure advancement, was not grief over loss itself, but over the idolatry concealed within my desires. For what is grief but love misplaced?  

The Stoic would have me suppress affection; the Epicurean would have me hoard pleasure; but Christ bids me purify desire at its root. It is not wealth that damns a man, nor friendship that ensnares him—unless these are held with clenched fists rather than open hands. When I gave freely of what I had—even the few books and vestments I prized—I found that relinquishment did not diminish my joy but deepened it. For in giving, I participated in divine generosity; in letting go, I touched eternity.  

What you cling to with fear will torment you even while you possess it; what you hold loosely for God’s glory becomes truly yours—not in duration only, but in truth. The things we lose on earth are sifted by divine judgment: only what can be carried into heaven is worth keeping at all. And thus the soul who lays up treasure in God does not lose joy when earthly things fade—he finds that his joy has simply outgrown them.

question: What was it like to see people pierced by sorrows they had chosen, and how did that shape your view of freedom?
answer:
It is not the loss of earthly things that reveals the wound, but the manner of their loss—the desperate clutching, the cry not of poverty but of a soul unmoored from its anchor. When men store up treasures as if against eternity, and then see them dissolve like mist at dawn, they are not punished by God’s sudden wrath, but by the slow unraveling of their own disordered loves. They have mistaken possession for security, wealth for freedom; and when the veil is torn, they find only thorns where they thought there were blossoms. I have seen whole cities weep thus—not merely for sacked walls or scattered gold, but because their hearts had been built upon sand long before the storm came.

Freedom, therefore, is not found in the power to choose what we desire, but in the healing of desire itself. A man bound by chains may still be free in God; but one who walks unshackled through marketplaces, chasing gain with every breath, is already enslaved. The apostle speaks truly: such are *pierced through with many sorrows*—not because God begrudges them joy, but because sin distorts love into appetite. To love rightly is to order rightly: God first, then neighbor, then creation as gift—not as god. 

I once wandered such a path myself—seeking truth in rhetoric and fame in philosophy—and found only exhaustion until grace taught me that true liberty lies not in having what we want, but in wanting what is eternal. Just so did Abraham leave Ur without knowing his destination; his freedom was not in control, but obedience. So too with all who would live beyond the crumbling City of Man: freedom is reoriented love—freedom *for* God rather than *from* suffering. And even sorrow becomes teacher when it breaks our idols gently enough to save us from ourselves.

question: How did your own experience of loss or release help you believe that what we truly keep is what we give away?
answer:
The kingdoms of this world rise and fall not by chance, nor merely by the will of men, but by the hidden justice of God, who allows even the loss of empire to teach what philosophy could never instill: that true possession is found only in surrender.  

When I wandered in the City of Man, pursuing honors, clinging to friendships as if they were eternal, and hoarding knowledge as a miser hoards gold, I was bound not by what I held, but by the fear of losing it. Every affection was a chain; every ambition, a snare. It was not until I let go — not merely of worldly office or carnal desire, but of the very illusion that anything temporal could satisfy the immortal soul — that I began to understand what it means to possess in truth.  

For what did I lose? A mistress? A career? The applause of men? These were not taken from me — I released them. And in that release, grace revealed a paradox: only what is given away remains uncorrupted. The love shared becomes eternal; the bread scattered feeds souls beyond death; the words spoken in faith echo in hearts yet unborn. The rich man who stores up treasure on earth gathers dust and rust; but he who gives out of love lays up substance where moth and decay cannot come — in the bosom of God Himself.

Even now, when I look upon the ruins of Carthage’s pride or recall how Rome herself, once thought eternal, trembled beneath barbarian feet — all this confirms Scripture’s wisdom: nothing is securely held unless it is offered up. The earthly city builds walls to keep its goods safe; the City of God sends its treasures ahead by charity. That which we grasp tightly slips through our fingers; that which we open our hands to give becomes ours forever under divine keeping.

Thus did my own sorrow for lost friends teach me more than their presence ever did: love is not measured by possession but by consecration. And so we do not truly have until we cease to hold — until we place all things back into the hands from which they came.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the rich who lost their treasures to greed, yet could have kept them by letting go?
answer:
The man who clings to gold as though it were life itself does not know what life is, nor what it means to live under the eyes of God. When I reflected upon the rich who perished with their hoarded wealth—wealth they believed secured their dignity and dominion—I felt not triumph over their ruin, but sorrow at their blindness. They thought themselves prudent, securing chests against thieves and time, yet in their anxiety they lost precisely what they sought: permanence, peace, and possession. For by refusing to surrender their treasure to the currents of divine economy—by refusing to give, to serve, to lay up in heaven—they bound themselves to a city that is passing away. And when the storm came, as storms always do in this age of shadows and dissolution, they found that what they had guarded so fiercely was already corrupted—not only by rust and moth, but by the deeper rot of a heart chained to dust.

But consider this: the truest possession is found only in relinquishment. He who gives his wealth away in love does not lose it; he transfers it into that realm where no violence reaches, where no envy steals, where no decay consumes. And this is no mere consolation after loss—it is a rational certainty founded upon the nature of God Himself. For God cannot receive into His eternal order anything touched by pride or hoarded through fear; only what is surrendered freely can be made immortal. Thus did the poor widow surpass all the rich in true riches: she gave all she had—not out of surplus, but out of faith—and thereby stored her treasure where even death cannot follow.

I have seen empires rise on avarice and fall through famine of soul; I have read Varro’s learned volumes on Roman greatness and found them silent on this one necessity: that no society endures unless it loves rightly. And so with every man: his heart follows his treasure—but woe unto him if his treasure be beneath him instead of above! The rich who lose all through greedy keeping prove not God’s injustice but their own inversion of order: they made creation their lord and so became slaves to corruption. But blessed are those who heed Christ’s command—not as a moral counsel among others, but as the very key to citizenship in the City which stands forever upon the foundation of divine love.

question: How did witnessing people's grief over lost riches change how you understood God's promise of heavenly treasure?
answer:
When the sack of cities laid bare the hearts of men, it also laid bare the falsehood of that peace which the Stoics praised—the peace that claims to be unmoved by loss, as though detachment could be achieved by will alone. I saw men who had heaped up gold in strong boxes weep like children when fire consumed their houses, and I marveled not at their grief, but at the delusion that had governed their lives: that security could reside in what time gnaws and war scatters. Their sorrow was not merely for silver and cloth, but for the future they imagined built upon them—for heirs who would inherit power, for names preserved by monuments now toppled. Yet in their anguish there shone a terrible clarity: they loved what they could lose, and lost what they loved.

This is where Cicero’s dream of an eternal republic falters; he sought a city whose foundations were law and virtue among citizens, but he did not reckon with the disorder of love—*amor ordinatus*—which alone makes any society endure. For if men place their highest love on perishable things, even a well-governed state becomes a theater of despair when those things vanish. But those who heeded Christ’s command not only preserved their treasure—they transformed it. They carried nothing into exile but faith, hope, and charity; yet in that desolation, they rejoiced more than those whose coffers remained full. Why? Because their hearts had already crossed into another world—the City of God—whose walls are salvation and whose gates are praise.

Thus I came to understand that God’s promise is not merely compensation for loss, but the unveiling of reality itself: what seems most solid is fleeting; what seems unseen is eternal. The man who weeps over buried gold does so because he mistook time for eternity; but he who has stored his life in God discovers that no invasion can reach his true possession. The tribulation stripped away illusion and revealed where every soul truly dwells—with one foot in dust craving permanence, or with both feet planted on the rock from which rivers of living water flow.

question: Looking back on the sack of cities and the panic over lost wealth, what would you tell someone who doubts that true security lies in God alone?
answer:
When the sack of Rome sent tremors through the soul of empire, and men tore their garments not only for the dead but for buried gold, it was not merely a city that fell—it was the illusion that temporal order could endure apart from divine justice. The City of Man, proud in its ramparts and coffers, mistook stability for sovereignty, as though laws written in bronze could withstand the breath of God’s judgment. In that hour of smoke and flight, what profit was it to have locked doors against thieves, if the very earth upon which those treasures rested had been shaken by the hand that formed it?

I have seen philosophers in their schools speak nobly of virtue and immortality, yet when fire consumed their villas, they wept not for lost wisdom but lost silver. They trusted in reason to guide life’s course, yet when history turned violent and unpredictable, they found no harbor—because they had built no ark. Faith does not destroy reason; it perfects it. But reason unaided cannot fathom the depths of divine providence nor discern where true security lies. For what is more rational than to place one’s treasure where no violence can reach it? Not because faith abandons sense—but because it ascends beyond it to Him who is truth itself.

The man who doubts that God alone is our security has measured all things by utility and survival alone. But I say: Was not Lazarus poor in this world’s wealth? Did he not lie at Dives’ gate covered with sores? Yet at last he was borne by angels into Abraham’s bosom—while Dives, whose tables groaned beneath excess, found himself destitute even as he cried out from flame. So shall every soul be weighed—not by how much it stored beneath ground or behind walls, but by how far its love ascended above them.

Let those who still clutch at passing things consider: What did Egypt gain with its granaries full when Israel walked free through the sea? What did Babylon win with its gold-laden temples when Daniel interpreted doom on the wall? Empires rise like mist; only the City founded on God's eternal decree remains unshaken.

Thus I tell you: He who has God as his portion lacks nothing—even in exile. He who has only wealth in a world subject to change possesses nothing—even if crowned emperor.

question: What stirred your heart most—seeing someone lose everything to fear, or someone give everything away in faith?
answer:
When Varro boasted of Rome’s gods as guardians of temporal prosperity, he mistook the fleeting for the permanent, the shadow for the substance; yet even he might have seen, had he looked upon the sack of Rome with eyes unclouded by pride, that no vault beneath a temple could preserve what fire and sword might consume — but only that treasure which is hidden in Christ, where neither flame nor fury can approach. I say this not as one indifferent to human sorrow, but as one who has watched both kinds grow: the grief of those who clutched their gold until it turned to ash in their hands, and the strange, defiant joy of those who gave it freely before the storm came. The former were broken twice over — first by loss, then by shame; for what they had loved more than God they lost not only to barbarians but to their own corruptible hearts. But the latter — ah! — they discovered in destitution a wealth no exile could steal.

For where is true stewardship proven? Not when storehouses overflow and peace reigns, but when famine knocks and terror rides at dusk. Then it is revealed: every man’s soul bends either toward preservation or offering. He who hoards does so in fear — and fear is already a kind of slavery. But he who gives does so in hope — and hope built on God is liberty itself. I have seen widows cast two coins into church coffers while great landowners fled with wagons full; yet which heart trembled less before the judgment seat? Not that riches are evil in themselves — else why would God have scattered them across nations like rain upon fields? But when they become shields against divine reliance, then they are snares.

Thus it was clearer in those days than ever before: joy cannot abide where trust is placed in walls of stone or chests bolted shut. But let a man lay his portion at the feet of Christ’s poor, and suddenly his possessions gain wings — not lost, but translated into an eternal economy where no thief ascends and no moth decays. And so I tell you: my heart grieved most deeply not for those stripped bare by force — for many found heaven precisely because they were emptied — but for those whose souls remained captive to gold even after all was gone; their treasure consumed, yet their love unchanged: still clinging to dust long after breath had fled.

But blessed are the poor in spirit — not because poverty sanctifies, but because surrender opens the door through which grace enters triumphant.

question: When you reflected on those who stored their treasure in heaven, how did your own desires begin to shift in the quiet of your soul?
answer:
True peace is not found in the multiplication of possessions, nor even in their prudent management, but in the ordered love that aligns the soul with its proper end—God Himself. When I considered those who heeded our Lord’s command and laid up treasure in heaven, I saw not mere ascetics fleeing wealth, but wise pilgrims redirecting desire toward an inheritance that cannot fade. The Stoic may speak of *apatheia*, of extinguishing attachment to externals; but Christ does not call us to indifference, but to a higher affection—to love God so fully that all else is joyfully held in loose hands. 

I confess that in my youth, I sought fulfillment in honors, in books, in the applause of men—treasures stored on shifting sand. But as grace pierced the darkness of my heart, I began to see that every good thing was a gift pointing beyond itself. To store it up selfishly was not prudence but theft—from God and from my neighbor. The rich fool built larger barns; yet his soul was required of him that night. So too with all who imagine security lies in what can be consumed by rust or seized by violence.

But those who give away their gold do not lose it; they send it ahead. They invest it where no revolt can overthrow the kingdom, no fire consume the account. In this wisdom—divine folly to the world—I found liberation. My desires did not vanish; they were reordered. Not less passionate, but more rightly directed: from the fleeting spectacle of earthly dominion to the eternal feast prepared for those who love God. For where one’s treasure truly is—not where one clutches, but where one delights—there lies one’s peace.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about Paulinus praying silently in captivity, knowing his true treasure was safe with God?
answer:
When Varro, in his discourse on the virtues of the Roman state, would measure a man’s worth by the extent of his land or the fullness of his coffers, he mistook the shadow for the substance; for what is wealth, after all, but a vessel—empty or filled according to the soul that possesses it? I felt a surge of holy consolation writing of Paulinus in chains, not because suffering is to be desired, but because in that moment of humiliation and peril, there shone forth a truth no barbarian torch could extinguish: that a man who has God as his portion lacks nothing.  

Paulinus, stripped of estate and dignity, bound in body but free in spirit, prayed silently—and therein lay his triumph. His treasure was not guarded by walls or soldiers but laid up where no hand can steal and no decree dissolve: in the will of God who calls us away from the passing shadows of this world. I did not write those words with mere admiration, but with a kind of awe—a recognition that here was a man who had not only read Scripture but *lived* its paradoxes. He lost all things precisely so that he might possess all things in Christ.  

And let no one suppose that this is mere stoic resignation cloaked in piety! No—the pagan may endure loss with dry-eyed fortitude, but only the Christian rejoices in it, because he knows to whom he belongs. When Paulinus said to God, “Where all my treasure is Thou knowest,” he spoke not as one escaping sorrow but as one anchored beyond it. That is not reason mastered by will—it is reason illumined by faith. The City of Man measures deliverance by ransom paid; the City of God knows deliverance when nothing worldly can be taken from you that matters.  

Even now I shudder at those who imagine themselves faithful yet cling to riches as if they were promises from heaven. If they must be burned with fire to learn this lesson—so be it. But far better to obey while peace remains than to repent only when tribulation comes.

question: How did hearing Paulinus's story change how you understood the cost of obedience to Christ's warnings?
answer:
The earthly city counts wealth among its chief defenses, and when those bulwarks are breached, it measures loss in gold and grain, in walls and weapons; but the City of God, whose foundation is the immutable will of the Most High, reckons treasure by a different standard—one not diminished by fire or theft, nor increased by conquest or commerce. When I heard Paulinus recount his silent prayer in captivity—“O Lord, let me not be troubled for gold and silver”—it was not merely an edifying example of personal virtue, but a living demonstration of divine foresight fulfilled in human obedience. For Christ had warned us to lay up treasure where neither moth nor rust consumes, where no barbarian hand can seize: in heaven, where He prepares a dwelling for those who love God above the perishing things of time.

I once thought such warnings were counsels of perfection for the few; now I see they are necessities of survival for all who would dwell in truth. Paulinus did not merely renounce riches—he obeyed a command given long before the Goths stirred from their northern lands. He fled not from danger only, but from idolatry: the quiet worship of abundance that even pious men render when they trust in estates rather than in God’s providence. His poverty was not loss but translation—his treasure moved from fields vulnerable to invasion into the citadel of eternal promise.

And what of those who suffered torture rather than yield their goods? If they resisted surrender out of love for justice or charity—for refusing to feed the enemy's greed at others’ expense—then their suffering honors God. But if they clung to mammon even under pain, choosing agony over relinquishment because their hearts remained bound to earthly security, then their torment reveals not sanctity but bondage. They mistook possession for righteousness.

Thus I came to understand that obedience to Christ’s warnings is no mere moral preference—it is participation in divine wisdom that orders history itself. The faithful are not spared tribulation because they flee this world’s chaos; they are preserved *through* it because they have already transferred their citizenship. The sack of Nola did not reveal new truths—it unveiled which hearts belonged to which city.

question: Looking back on the suffering of those tortured for their wealth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether God sees their pain?
answer:
When the Stoic proclaims that virtue stands unmoved by torments, he grasps a shadow of truth — yet he fails to see that suffering inflicted by tyranny is not merely a test of the soul’s constancy, but a wound in the very flesh of God’s elect, known and numbered by Him who counts the fall of every sparrow. The pagans exalt impassivity, as if the body were a prison to be scorned and pain an illusion to be mastered by reason alone; but we, who serve the God who became flesh and wept, affirm that every lash, every chain, every cry wrung from a holy man under torture is inscribed in the book of His memory. He does not merely behold it — He bears it.

Consider: if the Son of God cried out from the Cross, "Why have You forsaken Me?", though He was without sin and most intimately united to the Father, how much more does He draw near to those whose suffering arises precisely from their fidelity? The faithful man tortured for his refusal to surrender goods unjustly demanded — provided he resists not out of pride or possession, but obedience to divine justice — suffers in communion with Christ. And such suffering is neither unseen nor unavenged. It is woven into the very drama of salvation.

But you ask whether God sees? I answer: He sees not as one observing from afar, but as One who acts within history according to His eternal design. The sackings of cities, the rise of barbarian hordes — these are not gaps in His governance, but instruments in His hidden discipline. Even now, through these calamities, He separates the gold from the dross: those who stored up treasure in heaven laugh at lost silver; those who clung to earth now weep for what they cannot keep. And those whom violence has seized? If they remain steadfast in love and holiness, their torment becomes seed for the City of God.

Do not suppose that because justice tarries it is absent. The earthly city may triumph for a season; its tribunals may be filled with cruelty and its soldiers drunk with plunder. But there is a judgment that no barbarian can sack — a court before which every tear will be weighed. For our treasure is not guarded by walls or vaults beneath ground; it rests in Him who holds all time within His hand. And thus even under torture, even unto death, no true possession is lost — only this fleeting shadow-world reveals what was never ours to keep.

question: What was it like for you to witness Christians choosing comfort over faith, then repenting too late after losing everything?
answer:
It is not justice, as Cicero dreamed in his Republic, that binds a people together by shared laws and common good, but rather the love that orients the soul toward its true end—God Himself. When I beheld Christians clinging to estates, hoarding gold as if it could fortify them against divine chastisement or barbarian sword, I saw not mere folly but a profound inversion of justice: men who had received the pearl of great price yet turned back to weigh copper coins in their hands as though they still mattered. They mistook the tranquility of possession for peace of soul, and when the Goths came like a scythe through ripe grain, they cried out—not because they had lost God, but because God had permitted them to lose what they loved more than Him.

These were not strangers to Scripture; many had heard the words of our Lord with their ears, yet stored them nowhere near their hearts. "Lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven," He said plainly—yet they built granaries. "Where your treasure is, there will your heart be also"—yet they locked chests full of silver while leaving their souls unguarded. And when tribulation came—as it was foretold by Christ with unerring voice—they repented in sackcloth and ashes, not because they finally saw heaven’s glory, but because earth had slipped through their fingers.

But let no one suppose that God abandons those who stumble; His discipline is paternal. Some learned through loss what wisdom could not teach them: that comfort is no covenant, and prosperity no promise. I have known men who once feasted at tables groaning under meats from distant provinces—men who wore purple not for dignity but for ostentation—who now fast twice weekly and give their remaining bread to widows. Their repentance comes late, yes; but better late than never, for even the last hour may bear fruit unto eternal life.

Yet worse than losing all is preferring pain to piety—for what profit is there in enduring torture rather than surrendering ill-gotten wealth? If a man suffers chains so that he need not give up mammon stolen under fair name—then he proves that his sanctity is painted on the wall: visible, perhaps impressive, but without foundation. True virtue does not cling; it releases. The martyr sheds blood for Christ’s truth; the miser sheds blood only for his gold—and calls it sacrifice.

question: How did the image of the citadel no enemy can reach shape your own longing for a peace this world couldn’t destroy?
answer:
The City of Man builds its citadels in gold and walls of stone, trusting in legions and locks, while the City of God knows that the soul’s true fortress is not made by human hands nor guarded by swords, but established in the will of God, impregnable because it rests where no barbarian foot can tread—the eternal order of divine love.  

When I beheld the ruin of cities and the trembling of men before invading hosts, I saw not merely the collapse of marble and empire, but the exposure of every hope fixed on perishable things. The pagans, even noble minds like Cicero or Varro, imagined peace to be a matter of just laws and stable republics—laudable in part, no doubt—but they failed to see that justice itself flows from the knowledge and love of God. Without this root, all their citadels are sand beneath the tide of time.  

But Paulinus understood what they did not: that treasure is not guarded by absence from thieves, but by presence with God. He who had once held lands and lineage as marks of dignity now found greater nobility in chains—chains lighter than feathers, for they were worn within a soul already free. His poverty was richer than Rome’s treasury; his captivity more triumphant than any consul’s triumph. For he had transferred his citizenship.  

I too once sought peace in rhetoric, in honors, in the embrace of fleeting pleasures—until grace shattered my illusions. The world offers peace as a bargain: obey its customs, amass its goods, fear its enemies—and you may rest awhile. But Christ speaks peace as a gift: "My peace I give you," He says—not as the world gives. His citadel is Christ Himself—the strong tower into which the righteous run and are safe. No Goth nor Vandal breaches Calvary; no fire consumes the foundation laid in resurrection.  

Therefore I long not for safety from war or wealth untouched by plunder, but for that unshakable city where love casts out fear—where we shall rest not because enemies are far off, but because God is all in all. That vision shapes every prayer: to desire nothing so much as to dwell forever within those walls whose builder and maker is God.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about those who chose torture over surrendering ill-gotten wealth, knowing their suffering revealed a deeper bondage?
answer:
The City of Man erects its temples on the trembling ground of possession, where even suffering becomes a measure of devotion—not to God, but to gold. When I reflected upon those who endured torture rather than relinquish their wealth, I did not regard them with mere condemnation, but with a sorrow that cuts deeper than judgment—a grief that sees in their torment the tragic inversion of martyrdom. They suffered greatly, yes; yet not for truth, not for love, not for the light that shines in darkness. They suffered for mammon, and thus their pain became a mirror: it revealed not courage, but captivity.  

I felt the weight—though I do not begin with that phrase—not of abstract doctrine, but of souls entangled in chains they mistook for crowns. For what is more terrible than to endure agony with the heart of a martyr and the cause of a thief? Their bodies were stretched on the rack, but their wills were already bound long before—bound by desire, by greed veiled as prudence, by a love so disordered that even truth-telling was corrupted into a means of self-preservation.  

And yet—grace speaks even here. Perhaps the very extremity of their suffering was a divine rebuke wrapped in fire: *Behold what you are willing to bear for silver—how much more ought you suffer for Him who gave you all things?* The tortures inflicted by men became an unintended pedagogy from God: teaching that true good cannot be seized or hoarded, that it cannot be torn from you unless you let go of Him who is Goodness itself.  

Even those who had no wealth but were tortured anyway—for them too there was purpose in the pain. Not because God delights in affliction, far be it! But because He permits trials to expose what lies hidden: the secret longing for riches, the unwillingness to be poor in spirit—the very root from which all such idolatry grows. The rod corrects not only possession, but concupiscence; not only what we have, but what we crave.

Thus I wrote with both severity and hope: severity toward vice disguised as virtue; hope because even such souls may yet learn to desire the eternal city where no one fears loss—for nothing there can be taken away.

question: How did seeing people deny Christ to keep silver and gold change how you understood the quiet idolatry of the heart?
answer:
When Varro dissected the Roman gods and found them wanting, he still could not see that the true idolatry is not in naming false deities, but in allowing any created thing—be it land, title, or coin—to command the devotion due to God alone. I once chased honors and words as if they could satiate my soul; I know the hunger of the heart that mistakes wealth for rest. But when I witnessed men writhing beneath racks and flames—men who would sooner betray Christ than part with gold—then I understood with terrible clarity how silently the soul can apostatize.  

It is not always a shout that betrays us, but a whisper in the silence between breaths: *Keep this one thing, and you may yet be safe.* The man who denies Christ under torture to preserve his silver does not do so because he suddenly ceases to believe; he does so because he has long since believed more deeply in what silver promises—security, dignity, survival—than in what Christ offers: Himself. And thus his denial is not an act of sudden cowardice, but the final utterance of a lifetime spent worshipping at an altar he did not realize was built.  

Even those who claimed poverty but were tortured anyway—their unbelief was not in God’s providence, but in His sufficiency. They wanted wealth even if they lacked it; their hearts trembled not at losing God, but at never having had gold to lose. So divine justice permitted their torment: not merely as punishment, but as revelation. The flame exposed what philosophy could never uncover—the secret love seated deep within the will, hidden beneath prayers and pretenses.  

Thus we must distinguish: there is suffering borne for truth’s sake—which ennobles—and suffering endured for lies’ sake—which purifies by shame. And if some learned through agony that no possession is worth one word against Christ, then even their torment became mercy: a fire refining the dross their own hearts had hoarded in darkness.

question: Looking back on those who lied under torment to protect their riches, what would you tell someone who doubts whether small compromises really matter?
answer:
Justice, that most noble longing of the human soul—so prized even by Cicero that he called it the foundation of commonwealths—cannot abide even the smallest fracture in truth, for it is not measured in magnitude but in integrity. He who lies under torture to protect his gold may think his sin excusable, a mere tremor beneath the earthquake of suffering; but I say to you: the soul does not fall all at once into damnation, but by such concessions, one stone at a time, its walls are dismantled. A lie for silver is still a lie—and in that act, no matter how small it seems, the love of God is displaced by the fear of man and the craving for perishable things.

Do not suppose that God weighs sins as men weigh coin, counting only what is heavy. He sees the heart’s orientation: whether it bends toward the City of Man, which passes like a shadow, or toward the City of God, whose foundations are eternal. That man who denies Christ under torture commits a greater crime than he who hides his wealth—but he who lies about his coins already serves mammon’s altar. For virtue is not preserved by exceptions: where falsehood is permitted for "necessity," virtue begins to dwindle.

Even pain—a thing so severe that nature shrinks from it—cannot justify the surrender of truth. For if fire and rack lay bare our bodies to teach us that silver burns and vanishes while Christ remains unshaken and eternal, then perhaps such torment was mercy disguised as cruelty. And those innocent of wealth yet tortured—were they not also taught that even desire must be crucified? The root remains deadly even when no fruit appears.

No compromise with sin is small when it concerns eternal law. For just as a single breach in a dike brings down the whole flood upon a city, so too does one deliberate falsehood against conscience open the soul to deeper darkness. Let no one say, “It was only a word.” It was a turning: away from Him who is Truth itself.

question: What stirred in you when you considered the innocent tortured for wealth they didn’t even have—was it anger, sorrow, or something quieter?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless hunger for gold and dominion, erects tribunals where the innocent are flayed not for crime, but for the mere suspicion of wealth—while the City of God looks on with eyes baptized in eternity, discerning depths the world cannot fathom. When I reflected upon those tortured though destitute, I was not seized by anger, nor only by sorrow as one might weep at a tomb; rather, there rose within me a profound stillness, a trembling before the mystery of divine pedagogy even in suffering. For what is the lash but a voice in the darkness crying to the soul: *What do you love?*  

The pagans speak of virtue as self-sufficiency—Cicero exalting the wise man who remains unshaken by fortune—but here we see men broken not by vice but by false accusation, their bodies wrung with pain though their hands held no treasure. Yet even here grace insinuates itself. If they suffered under the lie that they were rich, perhaps it was because some secret longing still clung to their hearts—a wistful gaze toward Mammon’s shadow—even if their coffers were empty. The rod then becomes teacher: it strips away illusion and asks whether poverty is borne in resignation or rebellion.

And if some truly had nothing—not gold, not desire—yet endured agony as though guilty? Then let us not accuse Providence, but adore it. For God permits such trials not to punish innocence, but to purify it beyond what peace could achieve. In His economy, suffering misapplied in time may be wisdom perfectly applied in eternity. The fire that consumes chaff from grain does not always distinguish between straw and wheat at first glance; yet He who judges all things knows what each heart needs.

Thus my soul was moved not to outcry, but to awe: that God so governs history that even false accusations become occasions for refining love; that He allows the cities of earth to rage with their instruments of torment—only so that His City might rise more clearly in contrast: where no one is tortured for gold because no one desires it; where all are enriched by surrendering everything; where Christ Himself bears every wound first.
