question: What were you feeling when you defended those who took their own lives out of fear, knowing how deeply such acts troubled the soul?
answer:
Cicero, in his *De Officiis*, taught that reputation and honor are preferred goods, but not to be pursued at the cost of virtue—yet even he did not fully reckon with the violence that can be done to the soul when the body is made an instrument of another’s lust. I defended those who took their own lives not because I judged their acts righteous—for no suicide escapes the taint of despair—but because I could not bear that Christ’s wounded sheep, already ravaged by the wolves of this world, should be cast out from compassion by those who sit safe in judgment. The soul that trembles before dishonor, and sees only two paths—defilement or death—is already besieged on every side by horror. And if, in fear, it chooses the sword over surrender, we must remember that such a choice is born not of rebellion against God, but of a crippled hope.

For what is modesty but the inward shrine where the soul guards itself for God? When that shrine is violated by force, its sanctity remains intact if the will does not consent. But shame—ah!—shame lingers like smoke after fire: it clouds even pure spirits with dread that they were seen as willing when they were only suffering. And so some thought to erase that stain with blood. They thought: “Better to fall by my own hand than to seem defiled.” But in fleeing one evil, they embraced another—for no man may deliver himself from death save by God’s command.

Yet I say again: let those who stand unshaken weep before they condemn. Let them recall how frail is flesh and how fierce temptation can descend upon the unwary. If Judas fell after sharing bread with Christ Himself, who among us can boast immunity? The City of Man exalts strength and scorns weakness; it demands perfection of victims as if they were combatants. But the City of God knows otherwise: she bends low to bind up what is broken, not to measure how far it has fallen.

Thus I offered mercy—not justification—for those who despaired. For where reason shows us the law's rigor, charity must teach us how to bear its weight without crushing the bruised reed.

question: How did your compassion for the violated virgins reshape your understanding of sin, weakness, and divine mercy?
answer:
Justice, true and unshaken, does not measure the wound by the weapon that inflicts it, nor the fall by the height from which one is cast, but by the disposition of the soul that endures it. When I considered those holy virgins—those daughters of the City of God—violated not by their will but by the brutal force of men who served the City of Man, I found my earlier rigor softened not by sentimentality but by a deeper understanding of grace and moral order. For too long I had thought virtue could be measured only in visible resistance, in outward acts of preservation; but here was a case where purity remained intact even when the body was defiled, where chastity dwelled in the will while violence raged in the flesh.

This was no mere philosophical adjustment. It was a movement within my soul toward what Christ Himself reveals: that sin lies not in suffering unjustly but in willing wrongly. The earthly city exalts strength, control, invulnerability—it scorns weakness and brands shame as guilt. But in the City of God we know that shame may dwell even in innocence, not because sin has entered, but because we are still clothed in mortality and retain a nature rightly revolted by violation. That blush upon a pure cheek is no mark of complicity; it is rather a sign that modesty still lives.

And so I came to see with greater clarity that divine mercy does not descend only upon those who stand unshaken against every storm, but especially upon those who are broken while holding fast to God within. To kill oneself out of fear—even fear of dishonor—is to usurp God’s judgment; yet who among us can stand so high as never to pity such despair? Still more honor belongs to those who stayed their hand from suicide—not out of pride or stoicism, but because they chose faith over panic: faith that God sees what man cannot discern—that He knows whether consent ever touched the will.

Thus did compassion refine doctrine without overthrowing it. Reason confirmed what Scripture proclaims: that no external act done against us makes us sinful unless our heart has assented. And this lightens nothing—nothing!—of evil done to others; for rape is always monstrous injustice, an atrocity born from love turned inward upon self until it crushes another’s dignity like clay under iron. But for those who suffer it without consent? Their crown is woven with both tears and holiness.

Here is peace: not because pain vanishes or shame departs quickly—but because justice has been reestablished within. Not ours—the world’s crude balance—but God’s eternal reckoning: where intention weighs more than event, where desire toward Him outweighs all corruption visited upon us from outside.

question: Looking back on the stories of those who chose death rather than dishonor, what would you say to someone tormented by the thought that their suffering might have been in vain?
answer:
Justice, as Cicero sought it in the Republic, consists in rendering to each his due—but how profoundly he failed to see that the deepest injustice is not done to the body, nor even to reputation, but to the soul when it is torn between fear and faith. And so I would say to one troubled by such anguish: hear first the voice of divine charity above the clamor of human judgment. There are those who, pressed by violence and horror, chose death thinking to preserve an inviolate purity; and there are others who, no less pure in will, endured violation without consent and lived on burdened by shame not their own. To both I speak with pastoral gravity: God judges not by what was done upon the body, but by what remained unshaken within the soul.

For if virtue dwells in the will—yoked neither to passion nor compulsion—then suffering unjustly is not sinning. Indeed, Christ Himself suffered under Pontius Pilate, not because He willed evil nor even permitted it in His heart, but because He submitted to a violence He could have avoided had glory required flight rather than sacrifice. So too may a soul remain steadfast when overwhelmed by another’s lust or cruelty. The flesh may be defiled—though never willingly—and yet the inner citadel stand intact.

And as for those who took their own life from dread of dishonor? I do not lightly excuse suicide—no Christian can—for life is God’s gift and must not be cast aside even for purity’s sake. Yet let no man judge their hearts whose breath has fled before judgment came. Who knows what flicker of faith trembled beneath that final act? Who knows whether despair did not masquerade as piety? We must leave them to Him whose mercy extends beyond our reckoning.

But you who suffer now with memory haunting your peace—do you suppose your endurance has been in vain? Hear this: nothing borne for love of God is ever lost. The martyrs were torn limb from limb and thought themselves forsaken; yet their blood became seed. Your suffering too may yet bear fruit you shall never see—but God sees it all. The City of Man measures honor by unbroken bodies and unchallenged names; but the City of God measures holiness by fidelity unto death—not death chosen hastily from fear, but borne patiently through grace.

Therefore rise up from this torment. You are not condemned—you are called. Not shamed—but redeemed.

question: What stirred you so deeply about the shame that lingers even in the innocent, and how did it change your view of purity and the inner will?
answer:
The City of Man erects its temples to honor the body’s inviolability, as though purity could be measured by the mere absence of stain upon the flesh; but the City of God knows that the soul is the true sanctuary, and that defilement enters not by what is done to the body, but by what is consented to in the will.

It was not merely a philosophical puzzle that moved me, but a wound in the very body of my people—the weeping of holy women who had suffered violation at the hands of barbarians, yet bore within them a scruple that no pagan moralist could comprehend: they feared not only man’s judgment, but God’s. They trembled lest some hidden delight—fleeting, involuntary—had touched their senses in the moment of outrage, and with it, guilt. This delicate shame, this inward trembling before divine omniscience—that stirred me profoundly.

I saw then how deeply sin had disordered human nature: even innocence was haunted. The soul, though firm in its resistance, though wholly unwilling in every rational part—still shuddered under a shadow cast by another’s lust. And so I was compelled to affirm with greater clarity what I had long believed—that virtue resides in the will alone. The body may be seized; it may even respond with motions not commanded by reason; yet if the soul does not yield, if it abhors what is done and would flee if fidelity allowed—then no sin belongs to her who suffers.

The Stoic may boast that his sage remains unshaken by external violence; but we Christians know more: we know that even when shame invades—as it did when our Lord was mocked and scourged—we do not measure holiness by outward composure or untouched flesh. We measure it by adherence to God.

Therefore I rejected suicide as both folly and sacrilege—not because I underestimated fear or pain—but because no one may take lawful vengeance upon themselves for another’s crime. To do so is to confess a false doctrine: that bodily integrity defines purity. But Christ sanctifies souls first; bodies are raised again only at last through resurrection.

To preserve chastity even unto death is noble; but to die fleeing one’s own suffering rather than sinning? That belongs not to virtue but to despair—and despair has no place in the City where God wipes away every tear.

question: When you wrote that the will remains unshaken even as the body is defiled, were you drawing from your own moments of temptation or fear?
answer:
Plato himself, in the *Laws*, dared to condemn suicide as an offense against the gods, yet even he did not penetrate to the root of the matter: that the soul’s integrity does not consist in the preservation of the body’s inviolability, but in its unswerving adherence to right reason and divine law. It is from this principle—not from any private tremor of my own flesh—that I assert what may seem harsh: that a defiled body does not defile the soul unless the will consents.  

I speak not as one who has stood untouched by lust or fear—indeed, I have known both, and confessed them openly—but my argument here rises above autobiography. The question is not whether I once shuddered at carnal temptation or flinched before suffering; the question is whether virtue can survive violation. And if virtue dwells in the soul—as Socrates rightly taught, though he saw only a shadow of its source—then it cannot be expelled by another’s violence. The will that resists remains whole, even when the body is made an instrument of another’s sin.  

You ask whether personal trembling shaped this doctrine? I answer: it was shaped far more by witnessing how Rome fell—not because its temples were broken, but because its citizens loved honor more than holiness, and feared shame more than God. When Christian virgins stood before invaders, some chose death rather than dishonor; others endured violation rather than commit what they knew to be mortal sin. Both acted from conscience—but only one path preserved both body and soul without self-inflicted wound. To accuse those who lived? That is to invert justice: for he who sins inwardly by despair commits a greater crime than he who suffers outwardly without consent.  

And so I return to reason illuminated by faith: no man sins unless he wills it; no soul is violated unless it turns from God. The body may be used as a vessel by another’s malice—but it is not *our* vessel when such things occur; it is seized. And just as God remains sovereign though tyrants rage, so too may the soul remain chaste though the flesh be ravaged—so long as it cleaves to Him alone.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about Judas’s despair, as if you saw your own shadow in his reckless end?
answer:
It is one thing to fall from grace through sin, and another to collapse into despair through unbelief—here lies the abyss that separates the penitent thief from Judas Iscariot. When I reflected upon that terrible end—the rope drawn tight, the body swaying not in sacrifice but in flight, the soul fleeing justice without seeking mercy—I confess a shudder passed through me, not merely at his fate, but at the mirror it holds up to every soul formed of dust and pride. For we are all capable of betrayal; I have betrayed truth with my tongue, my lusts, my ambitions long before I knew Christ’s name upon my lips. But Judas did not merely betray; he despaired. And in that despair, he denied not only forgiveness, but God Himself.

The City of Man measures justice with finality: crime demands punishment, guilt requires expiation—immediate, self-wrought. It knows no patience beyond this life; it trusts no judgment but its own. Therein lies its folly. The Stoic may commend suicide as an escape from dishonor; Cato lifts his dagger and is praised by Roman tongues as if he had conquered fate. But what is such courage if it flees the living God? Where is wisdom if it refuses to wait upon divine mercy? No—our wound cannot be dressed by deeper violence. Sin multiplies when met with suicide; death by one’s own hand does not cleanse but defiles.

I write these things not only as a bishop defending doctrine, but as a man who once sought to master his life apart from grace—as one who wandered through the gardens of rhetoric and pleasure believing himself lord of his soul. Only when I ceased running did I hear the voice calling me back: *Tolle, lege*. Take up and read. Not destroy yourself in sorrow—but return.

Judas would not return. He flung back the silver and thought silver could balance blood; he hanged himself and thought death could end debt. But some debts are beyond coin or corpse—they are paid only in tears that open into trust.

So when I speak of Judas, I do not speak coldly. I speak with dread—and hope: that even at the hour of death, had he cried out like the thief beside Christ—*Remember me*—the same voice that said *Father forgive them* would have remembered him too.

question: How did your mother Monica’s faith in suffering change how you understood God’s presence in our darkest choices?
answer:
The City of Man, in its desperation, devises suicide as an escape from suffering, imagining the body’s ruin to be the end of the soul’s trial; but the City of God, taught by tears and fidelity, knows that even in chains and sorrow, the hand of God holds fast the righteous—not to spare them pain, but to sanctify it.  

I once thought suffering a silence from heaven, a sign of abandonment. Before my mother Monica’s deathbed, I had known grief as confusion—as when she wept for my wayward years, not knowing that her prayers were already answered in the economy of divine mercy. But in her final days, when sickness bound her to a pallet in Ostia and neither flight nor medicine could deliver her, I saw not resignation but triumph: she spoke of eternal life not as a hope deferred but as a home drawing near. She did not clutch at health as if this world were the stage of final justice; rather, she offered her pain to God as an oblation.

She taught me that suffering borne in faith is no defeat for God’s presence—it is its revelation. The Manicheans said pain was proof of evil’s dominion; the Stoics said one must crush feeling beneath reason; but Monica showed me something far greater: that love could endure and even welcome affliction because it trusts the Giver more than it fears the gift withdrawn. When she gasped her last breath on foreign soil, far from her African home, I wept—not because she was lost, but because I was still so tethered to earth.

In that moment I understood: God does not meet us only in deliverance from darkness, but often *as* light *within* it. To flee suffering by self-slaughter—like Judas seeking to end shame through death—is to deny divine providence mastery over both sin and sorrow. But to suffer with Monica’s faith is to confess that even our dying is not our own act of will, but a passage into hands already outstretched.

question: Looking back on your own temptations toward self-mastery and pride, what would you tell someone who believes they must erase themselves to escape shame?
answer:
The City of Man builds altars to self-annihilation when shame becomes too loud, mistaking the knife of self-destruction for the sword of justice; but the City of God raises up confessors, not suicides, for it knows that no guilt—neither another’s nor one’s own—can void the soul’s tenure under the sovereignty of God.

You who would erase yourself to flee disgrace, hear this: pride does not end in self-hatred, but often begins there. The desire to vanish is not humility—it is despair clothed in false virtue. You suppose yourself so burdened by shame that only your absence can set things right. But what is this if not the final arrogance? That you alone must bear the weight, that you alone must impose the sentence, that your hands—not God’s mercy—must deliver the verdict?

I have known such darkness. In my youth I sought mastery over my life through philosophy and willpower, thinking myself lord of my soul. When lust clung to me like a chain I could not break, I cried out: “Give me chastity—but not yet.” And later, when conscience smote me for wasted years and hollow ambitions, I felt the crushing load of time misspent. Yet had I taken my life then—not from despair like Judas, perhaps, but from weary shame—I would have committed a deeper injustice: rebellion against Him who numbers even our tears.

For no man may claim dominion over his own death. As it is unlawful to kill another without divine or lawful authority—even if he be guilty—how much more unlawful is it to usurp God’s judgment by extinguishing His image in oneself? The body is not ours to destroy; it belongs to Christ. The soul is not ours to surrender in panic; it belongs to its Maker.

True penitence does not flee shame by violence—it bears shame into the light where grace can heal it. Did Peter weep bitterly? He did. Did he betray his Lord with oaths and curses? He did. But he did not hang himself. He lived to be restored, to feed Christ’s sheep, to stretch out his hands in martyrdom at last—not fleeing sin by greater sin—but offering up his life freely in love.

Despair says: “You are beyond forgiveness.” Pride whispers: “Only you can fix this.” But faith declares: “God justifies the ungodly.” There is room even now for healing because Christ opened His side and made a harbor for all shipwrecked souls.

Do not seek absolution in oblivion. Let God judge you—and let Him pardon you too—for He judges with mercy where men see only condemnation.

question: When you condemned self-killing as a homicide of the soul, were you confronting a part of yourself that once valued control over surrender?
answer:
It is a mark of the disordered city within—the soul fractured by pride—that it imagines deliverance through mastery rather than through submission, as though the hand that strikes itself could absolve what only grace can heal. This is not merely a judgment on action, but a diagnosis of the will: when one seizes even death as an instrument of autonomy, he does not escape violence—he enacts it upon the most sacred vessel entrusted to him. I need not look far into the pagan wisdom of the Stoics, who praised self-murder as an exit from dishonor or pain; their virtue collapses under the weight of its own contradiction, for no man becomes just by committing injustice upon himself. Cicero speaks nobly of natural law and the harmony of duties, yet where in his philosophy is there a remedy for despair? Where is there room for repentance when the sword is sheathed in one’s own breast before mercy can draw near?

I speak not from idle speculation but from memory—memory not only of my own restless youth, in which I sought to command my fate through rhetoric, ambition, power, but even more so through that inner tyranny that says: *I alone shall decide when I am beyond repair*. There was a time when I valued clarity over humility, control over confession. And had I been pressed then to justify extremity under persecution or shame, who knows what fatal sophistry I might have forged? But God drew me slowly into a truth where even suffering has purpose within His City—not because pain is good, but because it may become an offering when joined to love.

To kill oneself is not to reclaim justice; it is to usurp God’s authority over life and death and to declare His judgment unnecessary. Even Judas might have found remission had he cast himself not down from a tree but at the feet of Christ in tears. But he chose instead to make himself both judge and executioner—an act so monstrous not because it ended a wretched life, but because it presumed no redemption could begin. And if such despair stains even one burdened with betrayal, how much more grievous must be the sin of one who destroys innocence under pretense of necessity?

No—peace does not come by our own hand. The City of God knows no suicide among its citizens; they endure injustice rather than commit it; they suffer exile rather than exile themselves from divine mercy. True freedom lies not in determining how we leave this world, but in trusting Him who governs both our entrance and our departure.

question: How did your grief over those who died by their own hand shape your belief that even in agony, we are not abandoned by divine love?
answer:
The earthly city, in its anguish, seeks to master fate by the sword—even the sword turned inward—while the City of God waits in patient faith for the Judge who holds life and death in His hand. It was not without deep sorrow that I witnessed souls, like those noble women of Carthage who perished by their own will rather than suffer violation during the sack of Rome, choose what they thought a lesser evil. Yet I saw in that very grief a mirror of divine order: that no suffering, however terrible, licenses us to usurp God’s authority over life. For what is man but a pilgrim soul entrusted with stewardship of a body and soul not his own? To flee torment by self-slaughter is not courage; it is despair masquerading as virtue.

I once wrestled with such despair myself—not by hand upon my throat, but within my heart—when long before my conversion I cried out beneath the weight of confusion and desire. But grace taught me this: that even in darkness so thick no star appears, the soul is not forsaken. The very capacity to grieve at all—to weep over sin, over loss, over injustice—is itself a spark lit by God’s breath. Despair denies it; hope submits it to divine healing.

Judgment belongs to God alone; therefore death must also belong to Him. When Judas hanged himself in remorse for his betrayal, he did not offer reparation—he sealed his ruin. His act was not penitence but flight from the face of mercy. And if Judas, guilty as he was, had turned back in sorrow trusting not in his own wretchedness but in divine goodness, might he too have found absolution? Who am I to say? But this I know: Christ offered forgiveness even on the Cross—to thieves and soldiers alike—and no sinner need perish unless he willingly closes his heart.

So too those who take their lives amid persecution or sorrow do so under a false illusion—that they control their final moment when they ought only surrender it. The innocent may suffer violence from men; they may be stripped of dignity and dignity's outward signs; but never are they stripped of God’s presence unless they choose separation through unrepentant despair.

To die unavenged is not to die abandoned. To live—or die—in hope is already to dwell within the walls of that eternal City where tears are gathered up and justice flows from love eternal.

question: What were you feeling when you defended the inviolability of the soul against the violation of the body, as if your own heart were torn between horror and resolve?
answer:
If Cicero, in his *De Officiis*, could argue that reputation and outward condition touch not the integrity of the soul, then why do so many who claim to follow Christ believe that bodily violation stains the inner man? Is virtue, then, so fragile a thing that it can be shattered by another’s hand upon the flesh? No—by no means. The soul is not a vessel spilled by external force, but a citadel guarded by will and intention. Purity is not measured by what is done to us, but by what we choose; it is a matter of love directed rightly—toward God—or turned awry toward self or sin. When the body is seized against its owner’s consent, the act belongs wholly to the aggressor: his lust, his guilt, his defilement. The victim remains—may remain—wholly unpolluted in soul, even as he suffers violence in body.

I have known darkness—known how desire can master a man before grace reorders his loves. And therefore I know with certainty: what defiles is not touch, but consent; not suffering, but delight in evil. The martyrs were stripped, broken, exposed—and yet their crowns shine all the brighter for it. Shall we say that Susanna lost her purity when false judges conspired to accuse her? Or that Joseph was tainted when Potiphar’s wife grasped his garment? Scripture refutes such folly.

Thus I speak not only as a bishop defending doctrine, but as one who once wandered through the crooked paths of pride and lust—and learned at last that no human hand can tear from the soul its allegiance to God unless it willingly lets go. To fear bodily violation more than moral complicity is to confuse the city of man with the City of God. In the one, honor lies in flesh and reputation; in the other, honor lies solely in fidelity to divine law.

Therefore let no one imagine that rape or assault casts stain upon the innocent sufferer. That would make virtue corporeal—a thing subject to fate—and reduce righteousness to fortune rather than will. But if righteousness dwells in the soul—if virtue is an ordering of love under God—then even beneath torture and shame, under darkness unspeakable, a soul may stand unshaken: loving God still; refusing consent; enduring as Christ endured upon the cross—not diminished by violence done unto Him but perfected through obedience.

This truth I hold without wavering: where there is no consent there can be no sin; and where there is no sin there remains purity—not as world counts purity—but as God sees it: inwardly whole because wholly His.

question: How did your own struggles with temptation reshape your understanding of purity when you realized it could not be stolen by another's hands?
answer:
It is not the body’s integrity but the soul’s allegiance that determines purity, and this truth I came to know not in speculation, but through the long war within my own members—the rebellion of desire against reason, the shame that lingered even after the act was done. I once believed that sin was measured by motion: if the hand reached, if the mouth spoke, if the flesh yielded—then guilt followed. But when I passed from Manichaeism into the light of divine grace, I learned that virtue is not a garment one can be stripped of by another’s violence, nor a substance that leaks away under compulsion. No—purity resides where God alone sees: in the will’s consent or refusal.

Plato taught that justice is order—the soul rightly governed by reason under wisdom. And he was not far from truth. Yet he did not see that this order is sustained not by human strength alone, but by divine aid against powers too great for nature. For who among the philosophers could explain how a soul may burn with lust it hates? Or how a man may weep in silence while his body performs what his heart abhors? I know this darkness well; it was mine in Carthage, in Rome, in Milan—where I loved and used what should have been revered and offered.

Thus I now understand: he who suffers violation at another’s hand does not lose purity unless his will consents. The body may be seized, as a temple may be defiled by sacrilege—but God dwells not in temples made with hands. So too, the soul remains intact if it abides in love for Him. The martyr endures torture without sin; why then should the violated be deemed impure when their heart cries out against the act?

Purity is no fragile vessel to be shattered by force—it is fortitude armed with charity. It triumphs not by escape from suffering but by resistance to complicity. And this virtue cannot be taken; only surrendered.

question: Looking back on the suffering of those you loved, like the violated women of your community, what would you say to someone who believes their worth was destroyed by violence?
answer:
If the soul governs the person by will and consent, and if virtue resides in the soul rather than in the flesh, then no violation inflicted upon the body can reach the seat of moral integrity unless willingly received—this is not merely my own reasoning, but a truth confirmed by divine wisdom against the whispers of shame that seek to usurp grace.  

For I have seen how sorrow clouds judgment, how the world counts honor as a matter of bodily integrity, as though purity were a vessel shattered by touch. The Platonists teach that the inner man must rule the outer, yet even they falter when faced with suffering; they exalt endurance, but do not anchor it in God’s eternal justice. But we know better: we who are baptized into Christ know that defilement is not caught like dust upon the skin, but chosen in the affections of the heart. The woman whose body is seized by another's lust does not thereby lose her purity, for purity is not a bodily state but a disposition of love fixed upon God.  

She has not consented—how then can she be guilty? She has resisted evil even in captivity—how then can she be stained? The City of Man measures worth by dominion, by untouched bodies and unbroken reputation; but the City of God measures worth by union with Christ, whose blood cleanses all defilement born of another's wickedness. To say that her value is diminished is to deny redemption itself—for if Christ has made her clean, who shall pronounce her unclean?  

I think often of those holy women who endured violence during Rome’s fall—not as sinners punished, but as saints tested. Their tears were known to God before they fell. And though men may reckon their lives spoiled, heaven counts them among the victorious: for they preserved chaste hearts while losing nothing that grace had given them. The devil seeks to convince us that holiness depends on circumstances; but faith teaches us it depends on allegiance—to self or to God. And where love abides in God alone, there no violence can penetrate—not even death itself can destroy it.

question: When you insisted that purity belongs to the will and not the flesh, were you wrestling with your own past desires that once enslaved you?
answer:
If justice is rightly ordered love—love that gives to God and to neighbor what is due—then the soul’s integrity cannot be overthrown by another’s assault upon the body, for justice in the soul resides not in its immunity from suffering but in its adherence to right order through willful consent. This principle I have drawn not merely from Scripture, though it shines forth clearly there, but from the very architecture of moral reality as reason itself discloses it: no man can commit sin *in* me unless *I* concur; nor can virtue be extinguished by violence from without, since virtue is the inner disposition of the soul toward God.  

You ask whether this teaching arose from my own struggle with desire—and indeed it did, though not in the way one might suppose. In my youth I was not only subject to concupiscence, but I *loved* it, I fed it, I made excuses for it under the guise of philosophy and freedom. I said to God: “Grant me chastity—but not yet.” And when at last grace pierced me, I came to see how deeply the will had been corrupted—not because flesh was evil, but because *my* will had bowed to lower things as if they were ends rather than instruments. It was then that I understood: purity is not a condition of untouched flesh, but a posture of the soul that chooses God even when every impulse drags elsewhere.

Thus when we speak of purity violated by rape or coercion, we must ask: Where lies the guilt? In him who acts against his neighbor with lust and violence? Undoubtedly. But in him who suffers and resists inwardly? No more than fire is blamed for being doused. The soul that does not delight in the act remains pure—even as a martyr remains faithful while his body is torn apart.

Do you see now why we must distinguish so sharply between body and will? Not because the body is unimportant—God created it good and will raise it imperishable—but because responsibility follows consent. If purity could be lost by force alone, then infants would lose it at birth whenever harmed; martyrs would fall before executioners; Christ Himself might be defiled by His tormentors. But such thoughts are blasphemous absurdities.

The man who fears such pollution misunderstands both sin and sanctity. Sin resides in turning away from God toward created things as final ends; holiness consists in turning back again—again and again—even after failure. That is why even those wounded by others’ lust may rise again in purity: not because nothing terrible happened, but because what defines them is not what was done *to* them, but what they have chosen *for* their hearts before God.

question: What gave you the courage to claim that virtue could survive bodily ruin, when every instinct cries out that such suffering defiles?
answer:
When the body is subjected to violence, the moral order does not collapse — for the soul, illumined by divine reason, governs itself not by what it suffers but by what it wills. The City of Man, enthralled to appearances and the dominion of force, judges defilement by the touch of corruption upon flesh, as though virtue were a garment that may be soiled by another’s hand. But the City of God knows that purity is not a bodily state subject to violation from without, but an inward adherence to truth and righteousness, fixed upon God who alone commands the will.  

Philosophers such as Plato have taught that the soul rules the body by reason, and that no evil done against a man can truly harm him if his soul remains just. We affirm this — yet go further: for grace perfects nature, and faith reveals what reason glimpses dimly. The martyr who endures rape or torture does not thereby lose sanctity; rather, he imitates Christ who suffered under Pontius Pilate and retained perfect holiness. If sin resides in consent — and all right teaching agrees it does — then where there is no consent, there is no complicity in the evil wrought.  

You ask why courage was needed to speak thus? Because human instinct recoils from suffering as from death itself; because pride cloaks itself in bodily integrity; because even in righteous minds there lingers a pagan dread that defilement travels not through will but through touch. But I who once sought wisdom in Manichaeism know how easily we mistake material uncleanness for spiritual ruin — until grace instructs us that God is spirit, and they who worship Him must worship in spirit and truth. To fear bodily ruin more than moral failure is to love the shadow more than the substance; it is to place one’s city among those who glory in strength of body or reputation among men. But our citizenship is above: there, no violence can reach; there, purity stands unshaken; there, even under torment or shame, the soul remains inviolate if it abides in love of God rather than self.

question: What were you feeling when you defended bodily sanctity as something untouched by physical violation, yet rooted in the soul's will?
answer:
When Varro, in his antiquarian pride, sought to derive Roman virtue from the customs of the fathers rather than the truth of divine law, he mistook the source of all true sanctity; and so too do those who imagine that bodily purity stands or falls with the mere intactness of flesh. The City of Man exalts the body as an idol, preserving its form with anxious superstition, as though holiness could be measured by membranes or guarded by untouched limbs. But in the City of God, we know that no violence from without can profane what God has consecrated within.  

I wrote not lightly, but from deep waters—having known the turmoil of a soul divided against itself, torn between lust and longing for purity. When I defended bodily sanctity as preserved by the soul’s will, it was not cold logic that moved me, but sorrow for those broken by shame unjustly imposed. I had seen women—chaste not only in act but in heart—despised as defiled when ravaged by barbarians; I had heard men equate virginity with physical condition alone, as though grace were surgical.  

How could I remain silent? The body is not profaned like a temple shattered by axes; it is sanctified by being offered to God in will and purpose. Even under violation inflicted by another’s malice, if the soul remains unmoved in its resolve to live chastely, then continence—true continence—endures. That is why a woman may be more truly pure after suffering violence than she was before it: because now her purity has been tested by fire and found unshaken.  

Do not mistake me: I do not deny pain, nor belittle suffering. But let no one say that God’s image within us can be erased by hands driven by lust. The soul that cleaves to God remains intact even when delivered over to tormentors; and so too does its holy discipline extend to the body—not through immunity from harm, but through mastery over meaning.

question: How did your own struggles with desire shape your conviction that continence sanctifies the body from within?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Officiis*, speaks of the moral order as rooted in nature and reason, he rightly discerns a hierarchy of goods—some to be pursued for their own sake, others for the sake of higher ends—but he fails to perceive that the true form of virtue is not found in natural integrity, but in the will’s orientation toward God. It is this divine ordering that transforms mere restraint into holy continence, and it is through such grace-formed will that the body itself becomes a temple, not by inviolate flesh, but by consecrated purpose.

I know well how the flesh wars against the spirit. In my youth, I wandered far from You, O Lord, not only in action—though that I did with a kind of restless hunger—but in desire itself: bound not merely by sin committed, but by delight taken in its approach. I was enslaved not only when I yielded, but long before, when my will luxuriated in the very motion toward lust. And thus I came to understand that purity is not a thing broken like glass at the first touch, nor a bodily state lost when another violates it. No—continence is a fire within the soul, kindled by love for God and sustained by His grace.

Had it been otherwise—if sanctity resided only in physical integrity—I would have had to despair long before my conversion. For I had defiled myself willingly; no violence from without had overcome me; I had surrendered from within. Yet even then, when my body was given over to concupiscence, there stirred within me an awareness: that if purity were merely bodily preservation, it could be destroyed without fault or recovered without repentance. But such cannot be: for virtue is an act of love ordered rightly.

Therefore I saw more clearly later what Scripture implies: that when chastity is rooted in devotion to God—when continence becomes an offering—then even suffering violation unjustly does not dissolve it; rather, such suffering may complete it. The martyrdom of virginity under rape or assault does not diminish its holiness; indeed, if the soul remains fixed upon God with unyielding resolve, then even under violence the body is sanctified by that very will which desires to honor Him.

Thus did my own passions teach me what philosophy could not: that no external event can profane what God has made holy inwardly—that continence is not fear of defilement but fidelity unto death—and that He who raised Christ from the dead raises up also bodies made sacred not by untouched limbs, but by an untouched heart set wholly upon Him.

question: Looking back on your journey from restless passion to disciplined devotion, what would you tell someone who believes purity is only a physical state?
answer:
If Varro could divide the things of Rome into divine and human, yet still bind them to earthly temples and fleeting honors, how much more ought we distinguish between the appearance of purity and its inward form, which no hand can defile nor violence extinguish? The man who thinks purity resides only in flesh imagines virtue as a vessel—whole when unbroken, shattered by contact. But I have learned through bitter wandering that the soul is not like glass, nor continence a mere barrier against touch. When I burned with desire in Carthage, when my youth was consumed by concupiscence and I cried out for chastity "but not yet," my body was whole while my soul lay in ruins. Therein lies the deception: to honor the skin while neglecting the will.

Purity is not passive preservation but active consecration—a movement of love toward God so firm that even bodily suffering cannot divert it. The martyrs prove this: virgins violated yet crowned, not because their bodies remained untouched, but because their hearts remained unmoved. The will to sanctity sanctifies; the intention shaped by grace renders the body itself an altar, even when profaned by another’s lust. This is no Stoic constancy nor Platonist ascent—it is grace restoring love’s order: God first, self for His sake, and all creation ordered in peace.

He who fears only physical defilement misunderstands both sin and salvation. Sin entered not through sensation but through pride; redemption comes not by isolation but by union with Christ. It is not intactness that saves us, but integrity—the wholeness of a soul turned wholly toward its Creator. Therefore let no one mourn as if lost what grace alone preserves: true purity grows not from enclosure but from election, from daily choosing God above self—even unto death.

question: When you wrote of the midwife destroying virginity while trying to prove it, how did that image crystallize your understanding of innocence as a matter of the will?
answer:
Cicero, in his *De Legibus*, supposes that law and justice inhere in the order of nature and the consensus of right reason, yet he cannot account for the inviolability of a person when the body is subjected to disorder and violence. If justice were measured by physical integrity alone, then every wound, every accident, every violation of flesh would diminish the soul’s rectitude—yet we know this is not so. The body, though noble and created good, is mutable; it exists within time and is subject to ruin from without. But innocence—true innocence—is not a mere condition of tissue or membrane; it is an orientation of the will toward God.

When I spoke of the midwife who destroys virginity while seeking to prove it, I did so not to dwell on a grotesque accident, but to expose a deeper truth: that which makes us pure before God cannot be taken by another’s hand. Virginity rightly understood—whether bodily or spiritual—is not a physical seal but a consecration of purpose. It belongs to the soul’s unwavering resolve to cleave to God alone, to refuse consent to disordered desire. Should violence be done against the body, that resolve remains untouched if the will does not yield. Indeed, such suffering may even become an occasion for heroic virtue when borne with faith and fortitude.

Thus, bodily sanctity is not negated by violation but preserved by intention. The surgeon cuts flesh to heal; so too may suffering enter the body without defiling the soul’s sanctuary. Just as fire purifies gold without destroying its essence, so divine grace upholds the soul’s purity through trials it neither sought nor desired. The City of Man worships wholeness as security—the unbroken wall, the untouched gate—but the City of God knows that true holiness survives even breach and ruin because it rests upon an eternal foundation: charity ordered rightly toward its Creator.

Therefore let no one suppose that God's judgment turns upon anatomical accident or carnal examination; He sees not what eyes inspect but what hearts intend. And where intention cleaves faithfully to Him—even amid wreckage—that person remains whole in His sight.

question: How did the idea that God honors the intention of the soul more than the fate of the body comfort you in moments of human vulnerability?
answer:
When Cicero speaks in *De Officiis* of the virtues as though they might be measured by outward integrity, as if the moral order were a matter of unbroken surfaces and visible wholeness, he fails to penetrate to the inner citadel where true virtue resides—the will ordered to God. This is not to cast aside philosophy, but to correct its vision: for no man, however eloquent, can fully grasp the soul’s dignity unless he sees it illumined by divine grace. The body, subject as it is to time, chance, and violence, cannot be the seat of ultimate sanctity; were that so, then every wound, every loss inflicted by another’s malice or mere misfortune, would diminish righteousness itself—an absurdity contrary to both reason and revelation.

It was this truth that became my refuge when I reflected on the martyrs—those holy ones whose bodies were torn yet whose souls remained unstained. How could it be that fire consumes flesh but does not consume purity? Only because purity is not a physical quality like health or beauty, which vanish with bodily violation. Rather, it is an orientation of the soul toward God—a fixed intention sustained by grace. When one resists inwardly even unto death; when one chooses continence not merely in act but in desire; when the heart remains unmoved despite what is done *to* it rather than *by* it—then sanctity endures.

In my own life, after those years spent wandering through lusts and false philosophies before finding rest in Christ's embrace at last—I came to see how fragile the body truly is. Yet I also learned that God does not reckon our worth by what has been taken from us but by what we have freely offered Him: our wills conformed to His love. Even in weakness—especially in weakness—the soul reveals its true city: whether it belongs to that realm where glory rises from self-preservation or from self-surrender.

Thus I found solace: for if the Lord honors above all things a heart wholly given over to Him—even amidst shame or suffering—then no earthly force can strip us of our true dignity. The body may be pierced as Christ’s was upon the Cross; yet from that very wound flowed salvation. So too does chaste intention sanctify fleshly members not through inviolability but through consecration—and even when profaned by another's sin, holiness remains where fidelity abides.
